•  R – The attribute is read only. It is not possible to modify its value.

•  R/W – The attribute can be modified in transformer configuration.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 126

Example – Copying of System Attribute to Normal Attribute:

In this example, the attribute #GRAPHICS_FORMAT# is not exportable to EIWM. To include the attribute’s value in
EIWM, you can create or re-use a non-system attribute and set its value to the value of the system attribute.

AVEVA™ Gateway for SmartPlant 3D
Gateway for SmartPlant 3D 6.1

<Attribute>
<Conditions>
<Attribute name="#GRAPHICS_FORMAT#" pattern="^.*$"/>
</Conditions>
<Name value="GRAPHICS_FORMAT" />
<Value value="[#GRAPHICS_FORMAT#]"/>
</Attribute>

In this example, the engineering attribute named GRAPHICS_FORMAT is added.

Appendix J: Limitations of the Gateway

Supporting Non-English User Locales

When the Gateway is installed on a system where the user has selected a non-English locale that uses a comma
as the decimal separator (that is, a number 123.45 in a Windows system with a French locale would be expressed
as 123,45), the Gateway does not interpret such numbering formats from input sources and configurations nor
does it export them into output files.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

linkedin.com/company/aveva

@avevagroup

ABOUT AVEVA

AVEVA is a world leader in industrial software, providing engineering and operational solutions across multiple industries,
including oil and gas, chemical, pharmaceutical, power and utilities, marine, renewables, and food and beverage. Our
agnostic and open architecture helps organizations design, build, operate, maintain and optimize the complete lifecycle
of complex industrial assets, from production plants and offshore platforms to manufactured consumer goods.

Over 20,000 enterprises in over 100 countries rely on AVEVA to help them deliver life’s essentials: safe and reliable
energy, food, medicines, infrastructure and more. By connecting people with trusted information and AI -enriched
insights, AVEVA enables teams to engineer efficiently and optimize operations, driving growth and sustainability.

Named as one of the world’s most innovative companies, AVEVA supports customers with open solutions and the
expertise of more than 6,400 employees, 5,000 partners and 5,700 certified developers. The company is headquartered
in Cambridge, UK.

Learn more at www.aveva.com

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

OCTOBER 2024

Introduction to Industrial
Digital Twin

An overview for new AVEVA World attendees

Presented by: Lora O’Haver, Director of Information Management Marketing

Exploring Digital Twin technology at AVEVA World
Check out related AVEVA World sessions

• Later today: Yinson—Get the Value from Real-Time Operations Data

• Infrastructure track; SESS-102
• Presenters: Kari Berte Bye, Manager Lifecycle Operations
• Wednesday, 16 October @13:15 – 13:45 |Amphitheatre Bleu

• And: Evonik AI--Another Digital Twin?

• Engineering track; SESS-99
• Presenters: Martin Blumoehr, Michael Resch; Asset Lifecycle Operations
• Wednesday, 16 October @14:00 – 14:30 |Room: 253

• And: Veolia: Smart Engineering Thinking in the Box

• Engineering track; SESS-260
• Presenter: Hakim Bouhali, Engineering Director
• Wednesday, 16 October @15:30 – 16:00 | Room 253

• From Day 1: AVEVA: Unlocking your Industrial Digital Twin

• CONNECT Innovation track; SESS-296
• Presenters: Daniel Crisp and Steve Parvin

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRIAL
DIGITAL TWINS

Agenda

• Challenges driving growth
• Definition of digital twin
• Essential elements of digital twin
• Use cases and benefits
• Real-world examples

• Success for BP’s offshore platform in Caspian Sea
• Success for Veolia’s water treatment facility bids

• The modular, scalable approach
• Final thoughts

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Digital industrial organizations: the new norm.

• Critical information and data

is generated in multiple
functions. (aka ‘silos’)

• Sharing information typically

takes time, effort and
technology/money.

• Digitalization increases

potential for connection and
the ability to squeeze out
delays, eliminate
inaccuracies and reduce
costs.

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

CapEx
Engineering

Data
scientists

Virtual experts

Plant

Day to day operations

Remote sites (e.g. mine, water treatment, power generation)

70% of C-suite technology
executives at large enterprises are
exploring & investing in digital twin
(McKinsey)

Challenges Complications being addressed

Digital twins offer answers to questions about interconnected systems

• Simulate traffic flow to optimize city layouts.

• Optimize first responders’ procedures in disaster situations.

• Optimize energy consumption across plant/ facility/

residence.

• Predict and prevent resource shortages (water, electricity,

etc.)

• Integrate and manage multiple energy-generating sources to

meet demand.

• Simulate agricultural conditions to optimize productions.

• Cities integrate digital twins into public safety networks to

enhance service response.

• Etc…..

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Essential elements of a digital twin

Digital twins are…

• Built for specific use

case(s)…

• …to serve specific

user(s)

• Composed of technology

solutions for:

Data

• What types of

data do you have
to work with?

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Modeling

• What can you infer

about what will happen?

Visualization

• What is it you
want to see,
examine, explore?

Essential Elements of a digital twin

Digital twins are…

• Built for specific use

case(s)…

─ Project handover
─ Risk assessment
─ Performance predictions
─ Compliance

• Composed of technology

solutions for:

Data
• Engineering data
• Real-time data
• Transactional data
• Third-party data

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

• …to serve specific

user(s)

─ Heads of Transformation,

Sustainability

─ COOs, CIOs
─ Heads of Engineering, Supply

Chain, Asset Reliability

─ EPCs

Modeling
• CAD/ CAM
• Behavioral-performance
• Supply chain
• AI/ML models
• Economic relationships

Visualization
• Enterprise-wide
• Location/ Site level
• Asset level
• Process level
•

Job role based

Digital twin use cases

Benefits achieved:

• Deliver on time and on budget

• Operate safely, reliably and

sustainably

• Improve product quality

• Increase efficiency and production

• Avoid unexpected costs

• Reduce time-to-market

• Seamless knowledge transfer &

collaboration

• Enable remote workforce

• Improve circularity and asset

lifecycle management

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Typical use cases

Key inputs

Digital twins in the real world

Organizations that are already benefitting

• Emirates Team, New Zealand
Digital twin of yachts, crew and
sailing conditions to test
performance of designs without
actually building them.

• PETRONAS Oil & Gas, Malaysia
Multinational company saved
millions and reduced its carbon
emissions using digital twin to
predict and resolve potential
equipment failures before they
cause a shutdown

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

• Exxaro Mine, South Africa

Built cloud-based digital twin of
their complete operations and
value chain. uses digital twin of
boats, crew and sailing conditions
to test designs without actually
building them.

• Global Brewery

• A global brewing and supply
chain digital twin enables
producer to adjust inputs
based on active conditions
and compensate for
bottlenecks that emerge.

Common issues with digital twin technology

High potential, but some factors can limit benefits

• Higher than expected complexity… particularly

when modeling existing assets.

• Poor data quality.

• One size does not fit all.

• Unexpected costs can limit or delay ROI; digital

maturity and data readiness is CSF.

• Know how to apply result and complementing

with real-world testing.

Source: Mary K. Pratt, “9 advantages and disadvantages of digital twin technology.” TechTarget ERP: 30 May 2024.

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Image credit: 12d Synery

OIL & GAS  |  AZERBAJIAN

BP optimizes design/build/commission of new
offshore facility in the Caspian Sea
Challenges
• Key sources of information for BP’s —laser scans, 3D design models and ….data—were

distinct from one another, taking time and expertise to connect.

• Complex decision making involved offshore and onshore activities.

Solution

• Worked with KBR to create the Azeri Central East (ACE) facility digital twin.
• Deployed AVEVA  Asset Information Management, AVEVA  Point Cloud Manager and

AVEVA  E3D Design

Results
• Faster remote access to data produced faster and more informed decisions.

• Cloud based digital twin solution increases collaboration.

• Better planned maintenance reduces carbon usage and increases safey

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Hear more at Veolia’s
presentation today in
Room 253 at 15:30.

WATER TREATMENT INFRASTRUCTURE  |  NEAR & MIDDLE EAST

Veolia uses digital twin to improve tenders and
ROI on design/build for customer projects

Challenges
• Stop recreating the wheel and leverage experience to accelerate speed and quality of

tenders/bids and improve project ROI.

• More accurately estimate CapEx and OpEx of project.

• Use real-time information to improve project designs.

Solution
• Implement digital twin using multiple, integrated AVEVA components: CONNECT
industrial platform, Unified Engineering, Diagrams, P&ID, Instrumentation, Asset
Information, PI System and Simulation.

• All tools accessible via the cloud to staff in any location.
• DT templates provide efficient starting point and drive Virtual Reality Simulations.

Results
• Engineering design for tender can be completed in 24 hours vs. previous 1-4 weeks, a

decrease of 80%.

• Optimization of design, build and operating processes expected to reduce energy

consumption by 15-30% and chemical consumption by 10-25%.

• Increased trust and satisfaction of both clients and company headquarters.

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Building a modular, scalable digital twin

Evaluating your strategy against the McKinsey model

• Do you have a full-featured, high
performance data foundation?

• Will you be able to seamlessly

connect the components in your
stack?

• Is your stack optimized for

industrial use cases?

• Is your stack optimized for scale?

• Is your stack vendor-neutral?

• Will you be able to expose results
easily to your key decision makers,
wherever they are located?

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Source: “Digital twins: The next frontier of factory optimization,” McKinsey. January 10, 2024.

Coming: your industrial AI assistant

The next level for industrial intelligence

• What if your industrial cloud platform could automatically propose actions to increase your operational excellence

and deliver sustainability?

• Could it give you a competitive advantage? Could you experience more “bang” for your “buck”?

• AVEVA Industrial AI Assistant is currently in preview with early adopter customers for a targeted general release next

year.

• What to know:

• Industrial AI Assistant is a digital chat feature that will be included with CONNECT visualization services.

• It will provide summarized responses from multiple data sources and will provide you with ‘citations’ to back up its

recommendations (visualizations and links).

• Users interact using natural language (based on large-language-models from Open AI and hosted in Microsoft Azure).

•  Data integrity and privacy are an essential part of its design. Your data is never shared with AVEVA.

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Final thoughts

The digital twin journey

• Digital twins are the culmination of advanced
and mature digital transformation… not point
solutions.

• Your digital twin applications will expand and

multiply with practice and success.

• Work with partners who have experience across

industrial technology solutions.

• Explore the advantages of connected industrial
platforms in accelerating ROI and maximizing
outcomes.

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

A digital twin is a digital replica of a physical object, 
person, system, or process, contextualized in a digital 
version of its environment. Digital twins can help 
many kinds of organizations simulate real situations 
and their outcomes, ultimately allowing them to 
make better decisions.

ABOUT AVEVA 
AVEVA is a world leader in industrial software, providing engineering and operational solutions across multiple industries, 
including oil and gas, chemical, pharmaceutical, power and utilities, marine, renewables, and food and beverage. Our 
agnostic and open architecture helps organizations design, build, operate, maintain and optimize the complete lifecycle 
of complex industrial assets, from production plants and offshore platforms to manufactured consumer goods.
Over 20,000 enterprises in over 100 countries rely on AVEVA to help them deliver life’s essentials: safe and reliable 
energy, food, medicines, infrastructure and more. By connecting people with trusted information and AI-enriched 
insights, AVEVA enables teams to engineer efficiently and optimize operations, driving growth and sustainability.
Named as one of the world’s most innovative companies, AVEVA supports customers with open solutions and the 
expertise of more than 6,400 employees, 5,000 partners and 5,700 certified developers. The company is headquartered 
in Cambridge, UK.

AVEVA Unified Engineering: Legacy P&IDs migration strategy

Standardisation is the Key for success

• We’ve decided as part of our evolution journey, that

standardisation is key for success

• Legacy migration strategy based on proven technologies:
• AVEVA compare / update  → 100% data migration
• DEXPI standard schema. → 100% Connectivity migration

• Using standards technologies would also:
✓ reduce development & support effort
✓ reduce customer configuration
✓ Simplify customer process & user interface
✓ Allow migration from various sources

© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

UEoCAVEVA DIAGRAMSSymbols & P&ID DEXPI Export OOTBAVEVA Next Generation EngineeringAVEVA P&IDSymbols & P&ID DEXPI Export OOTBAI/ML Drawing Recognition(Partners - DEXPI Output)CompetitorsIntelligent P&IDDEXPISYMBOLIMPORTERDEXPIP&IDIMPORTERSymbols PalettesP&ID InstanceDEXPIP&IDExporterAVEVA DIAGRAMSSymbols & P&ID DEXPI Export OOTBUE on LineAVEVA ENGINEERINGCLOUD to CLOUD XML File Transfer AVEVA Unified Engineering: Legacy P&IDs migration strategy

please refer to their products documentation.

❑ Tag format

be defined and mapped into NAM and Project to
allow ENGITEM creation during P&ID Instance
import step.

❑ Configuration

• We have implemented facilities into AVEVA Engineering
configuration module to allow classes and attributes
mapping using mapping form to facilitate data migration.
• As symbols are assigned to classes in conceptual model,
mapping DEXPI classes with conceptual classes will map
symbols.

• Some improvement are ongoing development as you

would see in the next slides.

ABOUT AVEVA

AVEVA is a world leader in industrial software, providing engineering and operational solutions across multiple industries,
including oil and gas, chemical, pharmaceutical, power and utilities, marine, renewables, and food and beverage. Our
agnostic and open architecture helps organizations design, build, operate, maintain and optimize the complete lifecycle
of complex industrial assets, from production plants and offshore platforms to manufactured consumer goods.

Over 20,000 enterprises in over 100 countries rely on AVEVA to help them deliver life’s essentials: safe and reliable
energy, food, medicines, infrastructure and more. By connecting people with trusted information and AI -enriched
insights, AVEVA enables teams to engineer efficiently and optimize operations, driving growth and sustainability.

expertise of more than 6,400 employees, 5,000 partners and 5,700 certified developers. The company is headquartered

Presented by:
Boliden: Insights of a conveyor
Greger Spetz, Technical Maintenance Manager, Boliden
Jonas Kemi, Partnership and Business Development Manager, SKF
Hans Otto Weinhold, Solutions Architect Lighthouse and Early Adopter Program, AVEVA
APRIL 2025
A century of 
exploration 
and innovation
On a cold December day 
in 1924, gold fever struck 
near the village of Boliden 
in northern Sweden.  
This marked the start of 
100 years of challenging 
conventions while 
providing some of the 
most important building 
blocks for modern society.  
4
About Boliden
• Provides metals made in Europe that are essential for 
the climate transition and modern society
• Conducts operations within exploration, mining, 
smelting and recycling
• Produces copper, zinc, nickel, lead, precious metals, 
tellurium as well as sulfuric acid
• Leads the industry in climate and sustainability 
performance
• Achieves world-class productivity through technical 
know-how and decentralized decision-making
Exploration
Mining
Concentration

raw materials
Metal production
Sales
Industrial production
The use of metals 
in society
Externally sourced 
primary raw material
Secondary raw material 
for metal recycling
Boliden in numbers
5
• Production of base and precious metals since 1924
• Five mining units and five smelters in Finland, Ireland 
Norway and Sweden
• Around 6,000 employees*
• Approximately SEK 80 billion in revenues*

Boliden
Boliden Corporate Presentation
6
Aitik
Copper, gold and silver
The world's most productive open-pit 
copper mine
Large volumes and rational methods 
mean high productivity
Electrification for climate-smart haulage
Milled tonnage: 40.7 Mtonnes*
Operating profit: SEK 1,419 m*
Average number of employees: 814* 
Aitik
* Financial year 2023
Our combined offering
• Bearings and units
• Seals
• Lubrication
• Condition monitoring
• Services
Founded in 1907
129 countries
40 000 employees
17 000 distributors
40 customer industries
Boliden and the Use Case
•
Eliminate risks of breakdowns in the conveyor 
belt system.
•
Critical Asset
•
Transport all ore from Mine to Concentrator
•
Bearing failure
•
Direct costs
•
Indirect costs, resources, spares, logistics
•
Production disturbances
•
Other unexpected happenings
•
Fire
•
Human errors
Business success objectives
10
Collaborative efficiency
Make vibrations-based data and insights 
available to broader audience like application 
engineers and maintenance planners. Sharing 
contextualized data for further analysis for 
both maintenance engineers and condition 
monitoring analysts.
Availability Improvements
The goal is to achieve a reduction in 
unplanned equipment downtime and 
maintenance activities on the critical conveyor 
equipment.
Data Quality 
Improved quality and efficiency of data 
analysis by having process and vibration data 
in a single asset view.
Using AI and ML
Improved asset status understanding by 
having process and vibration data in a single 
asset view. Perform analytics using ML Models 
on datasets to get pro-active alerts to 
operators and maintenance technicians.
11
What are we talking about?
Use cases during the Lighthouse project
© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
12
Use Case 1 - Collaboration enablement
1. Combining and Contextualizing data from BOLIDEN PI System and SKF Observer
2. Centralizing data in a global cloud-hosted repository to allow access for different 
stakeholders (BOLIDEN and SKF engineers can look at the same data at the same time)
3. Sharing data from Operations and Condition Monitoring between BOLIDEN and SKF
•
Make data available to from BOLIDEN PI System to SKF PI System using CDS
•
Data analytics (AVEVA Advanced Analytics)
Use Case 2 - Digital maintenance using AVEVA Advanced Analytics
•
Using Anomaly Detection Model to track equipment performance and vibration data 
anomalies
Use Case 2a – Gain Insights 
•
Enable BOLIDEN and SKF experts to better collaborate on a regular basis
Success criteria 
© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
13
Rapid Data 
Integration from On-
Premise to Cloud
Contextualization of 
Vibration Data and 
Process Data
Implementation of 
AVEVA Advanced 
Analytics to find 
Anomalies in process 
and vibration data
Improve overall 
equipment 
availability and 
minimize downtime
Achieve rapid 
deployment and 
scale-up to 
production
Solution Architecture
Why Connect SKF Observer and PI 
•
By using vibration-based the refined vibration data (MCIs) is made available for also non 
vibration experts both from SKF and customer. This increases value created by condition 
monitoring by improved utilisation of technical competence.
•
The combination of vibration-based MCIs and other operational data (“process data”) creates 
new insights and improve quality and efficiency of condition monitoring.
•
PI make vibration data available for “non-maintenance” applications and hence increase value 
created from the data.
•
Data consolidated in PI enables use of ML/AI applications.
• SKF Observer Connector to the AVEVA PI System is based on 
Dianomic FogLAMP. It has been developed in collaboration with 
AVEVA.
• The connector uses the existing Phoenix REST API in Observer.
• Historical data from Observer DB can be made available to PI 
• In combination with vibration data, the connector also transfers asset 
data structure from Observer making the hierarchy available in PI 
Asset Framework. 
• Possible to deploy on-prem, cloud or hybrid setup.
• No-code configuration. The goal is that “IT-competencies” will only 
be needed for initial setup
© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
SKF Observer Connector to the AVEVA PI 
System

SKF

Analyse

T

t

Step 2 – Build equipment dashboards 
for maintenance people and bearing 
application engineers
© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Bearing temperature, blue-left, red-right

Asset Dashboard for BT1490 and AI Assistant

Step 3 – Implementation of threads and 
models based on visual analysis in AAA
© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Threads and Anomaly Model in AVEVA Advanced Analytics
© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Step 4 – Data sharing – PI Cloud Connect 
next generation
© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
CONNECT to PI Agent now supports community streams
Sharing Data between BOLIDEN and SKF
© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
BOLIDEN PI System
SKF PI System
27
CONNECT enables BOLIDEN and SKF
to accomplish smart Maintenance
Challenges 
•
Low availability and high maintenance cost on critical belt conveyors resulting in high TCO and low 
productivity.
•
Conveyor Belts have currently high failure rates on bearings – causing unplanned downtime
•
BOLIDEN Maintenance Engineers traditionally only worked with a limited dataset from SCADA
•
SKF Vibration analysts only looked on Condition Monitoring data from their expert system (SKF Observer) – no 
process context
Solution
•
Integration and contextualization of SCADA and SKF Observer data in BOLIDEN PI System
•
Leverage CONNECT Data Services as industrial data enablement infrastructure 
•
Continuous Data Sharing between BOLIDEN and SKF
•
Leverage AVEVA Advanced Analytics for rapid implementation of self-service data analytics
•
Partnership approach: Client – Solution Partner - Vendor
Results
•
End-to-End data availability
•
Connecting process with condition monitoring data empowers collaboration
•
Gaining new insights on both ends (BOLIDEN Maintenance Engineer and SKF Vibration Data Analysts)
•
Potential reduction by of Downtime as a result by being able to react on equipment behaviour proactively
The CONNECT platform enables BOLIDEN 
Maintenance engineers to collaborate 
with SKF application engineers and get 
better insights by combining Condition 
Monitoring data from our PI System and 
the SKF Observer platform. This will allow 
to reduce unplanned downtime 
significantly.
Greger Spetz, BOLIDEN, Technical 
Maintenance Manager
Lessons learned
28
Process to discuss and create insights for 
the people on the equipment from the 
different experts across BOLIDEN and SKF
2

ABOUT AVEVA 
AVEVA is a world leader in industrial software, providing engineering and operational solutions across multiple industries, 
including oil and gas, chemical, pharmaceutical, power and utilities, marine, renewables, and food and beverage. Our 
agnostic and open architecture helps organizations design, build, operate, maintain and optimize the complete lifecycle 
of complex industrial assets, from production plants and offshore platforms to manufactured consumer goods.
Over 20,000 enterprises in over 100 countries rely on AVEVA to help them deliver life’s essentials: safe and reliable 
energy, food, medicines, infrastructure and more. By connecting people with trusted information and AI-enriched 
insights, AVEVA enables teams to engineer efficiently and optimize operations, driving growth and sustainability.
Named as one of the world’s most innovative companies, AVEVA supports customers with open solutions and the 
expertise of more than 6,400 employees, 5,000 partners and 5,700 certified developers. The company is headquartered 
in Cambridge, UK.
Learn more at www.aveva.com

ABOUT AVEVA

AVEVA is a world leader in industrial software, providing engineering and operational solutions across multiple industries,
including oil and gas, chemical, pharmaceutical, power and utilities, marine, renewables, and food and beverage. Our
agnostic and open architecture helps organizations design, build, operate, maintain and optimize the complete lifecycle
of complex industrial assets, from production plants and offshore platforms to manufactured consumer goods.

Over 20,000 enterprises in over 100 countries rely on AVEVA to help them deliver life’s essentials: safe and reliable
energy, food, medicines, infrastructure and more. By connecting people with trusted information and AI -enriched
insights, AVEVA enables teams to engineer efficiently and optimize operations, driving growth and sustainability.

Named as one of the world’s most innovative companies, AVEVA supports customers with open solutions and the
expertise of more than 6,400 employees, 5,000 partners and 5,700 certified developers. The company is headquartered
in Cambridge, UK.

# SOURCE: vendor_aveva\Bruce Power_  Powering the Future Together with AVEVA.pdf
---

Bruce Power

Powering the
Future with AVEVA

AVEVA World 2024

What is Bruce Power

Bruce Power generates up to 6,550 Mega
Watts (MW) from eight CANDU units to power
30 per cent of Ontario

Through the Life Extension program six units
are undergoing refurbishments to provide clean
energy for decades to come

Fighting cancer through the production of
medical isotopes Lutetium 177 and Cobalt 60,
used against prostate cancer, brain tumors
and more

Exploring a new opportunity for another large
nuclear station on the Bruce Power site

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   3

Securing our future

• Bruce Power’s future is only secured if we operate safely,
reliably and deliver each Unit’s refurbishment faster and
more affordably than the last

• So, Bruce Power is moving to data centric processes using
modern, industry best solutions. A digital transformation

Targets

 50% Data retrieval savings

 25% Engineering modification savings

 10% Field execution (installation) savings

 80% Project handover / turnover savings

The Unit 6
refurbishment took
thousands working
24 million hours to
complete on time
and on budget

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   4

Our strategy

• Bruce Power needed a standard solution and a partner

that can help our digital transformation journey

• Started with deploying AVEVA Asset Information

Management (AIM) and Point Cloud Manager (PCM).

• Benchmarked with existing AVEVA partners

• Configure the AVEVA Unified Engineering tools and
focused on transitioning project specific data first

• Move 1D data to AVEVA Engineering, move subsets of

2D data to AVEVA P&ID and Electrical & Instrumentation,
enabled Everything 3D for projects to manage 3D models

• Validated new work-flows and configuration through

execution of pilot projects with key Engineering Vendors

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   5

Deploying AVEVA AIM – Setting the Foundation

Key features we leveraged from Asset
Information Management (AIM):

• Class Library and hierarchy built within data model

in Information Standards Manager (ISM)

• Data Extractor Gateway: Document scraping to

create document-to-tag relationships

• AutoCAD 2D Gateway: Digitize flowsheets,
enabling interactive drawings to support plant
status control and data retrieval

• Registers Gateway: Push multiple data sets from

current Enterprise Asset Management

• Enhanced search capability: search across

multiple systems in one place

• Create new relationships to provide new insights

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   6

Deployed AIM for Enterprise Use
Moved, digitized and integrated:

•
•
•

•
•
•

720,000 equipment codes (i.e., tags)
4,000 operational flowsheet drawings
750,000 documents (drawings, technical specifications, manuals,
reports)
1,000,000 + records
30 + data sets including work orders, work protection, catalogue data
40,000 Plant Information tags

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   7

Results of AVEVA Asset Information Management – Early Wins

• More than 1800 unique users, 1050 repeat

users to save more than 1000 hours to date

• Connects information from five systems into one

• Digital flowsheets act as a key entry point into
finding data in the tool. Tag hot-spotting at
approximately 60 to 80% across all
Flowsheets.

• Automated document-to-tag relationships to
remove a manually intensive process, > 50%
savings on cost and schedule at Engineering
Change close-out

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   8

Results of AVEVA Asset Information Management - People

Change agents

During the rollout of Asset
Information Management (AIM)
we found numerous ‘change
agents’ within our organization
who’ve taken it upon themselves
to advocate for the search tool.

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   9

Results of AVEVA Asset Information Management - Summary

Highest value:
• Document-to-tag
• Digital Flowsheets
• Search
• Link to Plant Information
for real time process data

What’s next with AIM:
• Markup feature to support training,

engineering, and projects
• Integration with Point Cloud
Manager for physical plant
visualization

• Project data from AVEVA Unified

Engineering

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   1 0

Strategy for AVEVA Point Cloud Manager

• Deploy AVEVA Point Cloud Manager (PCM) in

CONNECT and ‘thick client’ for admins

• Defined laser scanning standard

• Focused on the refurbishment projects

• Defined equipment tagging standards in PCM for

connection to AIM

• Finding and empowering change agents

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   1 1

Results of AVEVA Point Cloud Manager

PCM Usage Metrics

• In 6 months, 7 of 8 reactor units scanned and
more than 15 projects have been uploaded
and are in use in PCM through CONNECT.
Approximately 8.5TB of data

• 900% increase in usage over the last three

quarters

• 30% savings from repeat walkdowns and field

checks

• 15% savings in execution from improved

design quality and assessing/project execution
planning

1000

900

800

700

600

500

400

300

200

100

0

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   1 2

Q4-2023

Q1-2024

Q2-2024

Q3-2024

Results of AVEVA Point Cloud Manager – Early Wins

Highest Value:

• Access for all: Enabled all Bruce Power access

• Visualization of hazardous and difficult areas:
The tool saved a project engineer ~ a month by
using PCM Centralized access

• Consolidated laser scanning data removing
potential repeat scans: Five examples to date
where repeated scans were avoided

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   1 3

The impact on our people

• AIM and PCM are new processes, so it was

important to change behaviour

• The focus was on the value and benefits

of the new tools

• Change agents helped lead the design,

build, and test phase of the project

• Multiple communication channels were

used: drop-in sessions, meetings, videos,
newsletter stories and emails.

• A standardized change management
process supported an effective roll-out

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   1 4

Nuclear Power | Canada
Bruce Power saves time to help secure its future

Challenge:

• Need to deliver each of the six-unit refurbishments faster and more affordably than the last

• Lack of modern, industry best tool and processes necessary to deliver this work

Solution:

• Deploying AVEVA AIM, AVEVA ISM and AVEVA PCM at an enterprise level for improved

visualization and tag centric data and AVEVA Engineering, P&ID, AVEVA E3D and AVEVA
ERM to promote full workflow of engineering changes and projects to brownfield data.

Results:

• Saved employees more than 1000 hours from data retrieval and connected data through

implementation of AVEVA AIM. Saved half the time of close out processes by automating doc-
to-tag relationships.

• Delivered the seamless integration of AVEVA PCM for almost 1000 employees to realize a

900% growth in users over the last two quarters. Shaved 15% off the execution of projects
through AVEVA PCM’s ability to improve design quality, assessing and project planning

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   1 5

Bruce Power: Powering the Future Together with AVEVA

P A G E   1 6

ENERGY / OIL / GAS  |  USA
• No field instrumentation installed for certain business challenges
• Installing traditional field instrumentation for these scenarios is cost prohibitive
• Data collection, sharing, and analysis performed manually or not at all 
• Time consuming and lower quality
• AVEVA PI Infrastructure, PI AF, PI Vision, PI Connectors, Seeq
• Low cost industrial field sensors for use in Electrically Safe/Hydrocarbon risk areas
• LoRaWAN wireless technology fit for purpose for Industrial use – wide area, few 
gateways = low cost
• Low data rate / battery powered – no power + network installation required
• .
• AVEVA PI Industrial Data Infrastructure empowers data analytics for enhanced 
visibility to operational business challenges
• Safer Operations
• LoRaWAN wireless infrastructure provides a platform for multiple current and future 
solutions at minimal cost / production operations impact

LoRaWAN Wireless Industrial Valve Position Sensor
26
Challenge
• Field work requires process lines to be removed 
from service and locked out for days/weeks, 
months depending on scope/scale of works.
• Many 100’s of manual valves that are not 
instrumented. No alert mechanism if valves are 
opened or closed until process conditions change
• Cost effective method to ensure manual valve 
movements are tracked and alerted to maintain 
safe operations
Solution
• Industrial valve sensors that track any movement 
of valve handles
• LoRaWAN electrically safe valve position sensor
• Position data sent as soon as movement is 
detected, otherwise polling interval
• PI Vision Screens to visually view current 
conditions and any unexpected activity.
• PI AF logic for alerting unexpected activity
LoRaWAN Wireless Industrial Valve Position Sensor
27
Quarter-Turn Valves
LoRaWAN Wireless Industrial Valve Position Sensor
28
Multi-Turn Valves
Conclusions & Lessons Learned 
29
BENEFITS REALIZED
• Modern Industrial Wireless Technologies deliver 
solutions to LNG Field Operations challenges at 
low cost
• $$ Millions avoided through modern Industrial IOT 
solutions delivered for $ Thousands
• Safer Operations
• Enhanced Production Opportunities
• Wireless data infrastructure is a powerful strategic 
investment. One installation unlocks multiple 
opportunities and use cases with current and near 
future industrial sensors and mobile devices
© 2023 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
CHALLENGES
Implementation Partners / Expertise for Industrial 
Digital Solutions is on downward trend and 
accelerating. 
 
- Difficult to identify and access expertise
 
- Limited resources are split across multiple 
 
 
projects leading to implementation delays
 
- Industrial Systems and Application Providers 
 
 
challenged to effectively service clients
Digital Change Management – Educating the 
organization on new technologies; taking everyone 
along for the ride.
 
- Prove with working solutions; then scale 
 
- Keep expectations low, overdeliver to 
 
 
 
counteract the execution challenge
Energy Industry digital participants must collaborate effectively with Technology Industry to accelerate solutions
Thank You!
© 2023 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

https://www.linkedin.com/in/franz-field/

https://www.linkedin.com/groups/14312452/

31

Fan motor predictive maintenance
Vibration + Acoustic
Ambient + Surface Temperature
Replace / reduce monthly high fidelity vibration 
monitoring round to lower frequency. 
Wireless Vibration sensor performance change 
triggers engineering / maintenance visit to 
inspect / conduct high fidelity vibration 
assessment
90+ fin fan motors per train x 3 Train = material 
reduction in monthly vibration round effort.

ISM Compare and Merge

Melinda Hemstead, Data Consultant

March 2021| Version 1.3

ISM - Compare

Within ISM you can import, export and manipulate your
standards

You can produce comparisons of Class libraries to look
for matches, modifications, deletions, missing data.
You can merge Class Libraries into one another

Comparing Class Libraries

We will compare a company Class
Library with CFIHOS for this
example

B

C

A

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Click on your chosen Class Library from
the menu.
A - Click on the down ‘Arrow’      icon and
choose ‘Open’ to make your Class Library
‘Active’
B - Once active click on the back arrow
C - you will see the arrow icon now faces
right indicating it is active)

Comparing Class Libraries

Click on the preloaded CFIHOS Class Library you are
going to compare with
Click on the down ‘Arrow’      icon and choose
‘Compare with Active Class Library’

ISM will highlight the new information and show what
it would delete/modify and add with this comparison

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Once you have produced your comparison, you
can work on it directly in ISM, save it as a new
version or export the results.
*You do not have to save your comparison.
**Once saved the colours shown will be lost

Comparing Class Libraries

Once you have produced your comparison, you
can work on it directly in ISM, save it as a new
version or export the results to study.

*You do not have to save your comparison.
**Once saved the colours shown will be lost

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Exporting a Comparison

When Exporting your results, check
the box/es for ‘Include Change
Tracking’ to enable the compared
results to be exported.
Export either the full Class Library
or only any rows that have been
affected by the Compare

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Export of Comparison

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

The changes are colour highlighted
and advise the differences between
the 2 Class Libraries in the column
‘_CHANGES’.
Green = Added
Yellow= Modified
Red = Deleted
By hovering over the highlighted cell
(where is shows a red triangle), the
comment will advise you the reason
for the modification. The same
information can be viewed directly
in ISM. To cancel the comparison
click on the cross to close the
results.
Note – This comparison is choosing
CFIHOS above the company content.
If you compare the other way round
it would choose the company
content over CFIHOS

ISM - Merge

A

B

C

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

You can merge multiple Class
Libraries or fragments of
Class Libraries together.

A - To Merge your Class
Library with anther Class
Library you need to ‘lock’
your library for editing
B - go back to the main menu
using the back arrow
C – Locate the chosen Class
Library and from the down
arrow      choose ‘Merge into
Active Class Library’

Viewing the Results in ISM

You can view the results in
ISM by clicking on the
Modification Filters and
choose to view all
modifications or you can drill
down and view the sections
you wish to study.
Click Search to bring the
results into view

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Exporting a Merged Class Library

To ‘Export’ the Merge, click on Export
and choose the AVEVA ISM Standard
Excel Format option.
Choose or remove options offered –
You must include ‘Include Change
Tracking to export the Merge.
You can choose to Export all of the
Class Library or focus on the changed
rows only

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Exported Results of Merge

Exported results show the CFIHOS
Class library has been added to the
company Class Library shown by the
_CHANGE column.
Green = Added
Yellow = Modified
Red = Deleted
Notes are appended that advise what
the previous cell recorded before the
merge where there is a red triangle
present.

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Cancelling/Saving  a Merge

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Save  - this will save the merged
information into the Class Library

Cancel – this will return your Class
Library to its former state

Save As – to save the information into
a new Class Library

ABOUT AVEVA
AVEVA is a global leader in engineering and industrial software driving digital transformation across
the entire asset and operational life cycle of capital-intensive industries.

The company’s engineering, planning and operations, asset performance, and monitoring and
control solutions deliver proven results to over 16,000 customers across the globe. Its customers are
supported by the largest industrial software ecosystem, including 4,200 partners and 5,700 certified
developers. AVEVA is headquartered in Cambridge, UK, with over 4,400 employees at 80 locations in
over 40 countries.

ABOUT AVEVA

AVEVA is a world leader in industrial software, providing engineering and operational solutions across multiple industries,
including oil and gas, chemical, pharmaceutical, power and utilities, marine, renewables, and food and beverage. Our
agnostic and open architecture helps organizations design, build, operate, maintain and optimize the complete lifecycle
of complex industrial assets, from production plants and offshore platforms to manufactured consumer goods.

Over 20,000 enterprises in over 100 countries rely on AVEVA to help them deliver life’s essentials: safe and reliable
energy, food, medicines, infrastructure and more. By connecting people with trusted information and AI -enriched
insights, AVEVA enables teams to engineer efficiently and optimize operations, driving growth and sustainability.

Named as one of the world’s most innovative companies, AVEVA supports customers with open solutions and the

3  Introduction
The purpose of this document is to define suggested best practices for the implementation
of the AVEVA Integrated Engineering & Design software solution within a multi-discipline
project environment. The document includes the following:

  A generic description of typical engineering and design activities together with work

processes in common projects.

  A description of 3 typical implementation options with one in-depth detailed

example.

  An Appendix providing explicit details and screenshots for the effective

implementation of the AVEVA Integrated Engineering & Design  software solution.
This should be used as indicative material to initiate a live project implementation.

Engineering is a complex activity, involving multiple disciplines, such as Process, Mechanical,
Instrumentation, Electrical, etc. to fully describe all components in a project.
All disciplines need to share information at various stages during the project development.
For example, process engineers have to provide pressure, temperature and hydraulic
information to mechanical engineers for mechanical design and sizing. Similarly, electrical
engineers require the mechanical loads to complete its design. These are just some
examples of interconnected, multi-discipline activities.

The engineering project starts with the functional description of the client (owner operator)
requirements. These are translated into PFDs and P&IDs, which give more details about the
equipment, lines, valves and instruments that need to be engineered and designed. The
details are typically tag numbers, quantities and topology (sequencing of items). In situations
of repeat designs or process reuse, customers would generally start with preliminary lists
(lines, equipment, valves, etc). The PFDs and P&IDs are developed at the same time or at a
later stage in the project development.

It is not uncommon for the 3D design activities to start at the same stage of project
execution as the engineering activities and the P&ID drafting activities or just shortly after.

Process, Mechanical, Instrumentation and Electrical disciplines do not evolve at the same
speed. They are on different cycles, but all need information from each other. Additionally,
an engineer or designer needs to be in control of the data he/she works on, and needs to be
aware of any changes occurring in the datasets created and developed by other disciplines
that he/she is using. And, the engineers, draftsmen and designers could be physically in
different locations.

Work in Progress

Page 3 of 13

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

This is the challenge of managing evolving engineering and design data of multi-discipline,
concurrent and distributed engineering and design teams.

In this document we will more particularly focus on implementation requirements for AVEVA
Engineering. Data sharing between disciplines is an important thing to be in control of.
Therefore, we will introduce and explain terms like versioning, data maturity and status
control.

The next section will describe three common scenarios for engineering and design phases
and how data is managed at different stages and levels. Following on, one of these scenarios
will be taken to explain actual implementation requirements using AVEVA Integrated
Engineering & Design applications, AVEVA Engineering in particular in this case. It is expected
that with this additional explanation, the implementation requirements of the other two
scenarios could be derived.

EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

4  Scenarios and implementation options
This section will describe three different scenarios. It should be noted, however, that these
are non-exhaustive and many other scenarios are possible. It is expected that these three
scenarios are the most common ones and that others could be a derivative of these in most
cases.

1.  Multi-discipline users on small projects

The first scenario could typically be used by small project teams or teams with multi-
disciplinary personnel. In these cases we anticipate there are little requirements for
tight control of change.

2.  Multi-discipline users on medium/large projects using Status Control

A second scenario will describe the situation where each discipline responsibility is
well separated and one person only works on data within one discipline. In these
cases we anticipate one can only work on Approved data from other disciplines. This
is typically to avoid people making changes based on unvalidated, Work in Progress
data. The defined Status Control criteria ensure the appropriate data is shared with
all other disciplines while preventing uncontrolled changes.

3.  Multi-discipline users on large projects within Design Authorities

The third scenario deals with adding additional control capabilities, essentially
focusing on additional data maturity. In this case, maturity refers to key project
milestones. For more information see section 4.1.3. This scenario is similar to the one
above, except that there is an additional possibility to control the maturity of the
data that is being shared with other disciplines. Usually this scenario would apply to
large projects, with execution times spanning multiple years, where it is important to
keep control of historical information, in particular at each key project milestone.
Companies executing these types of projects can be referred to as Design Authorities
(typically in the nuclear, naval or car manufacturing industries). They still have a
responsibility beyond project handover to operations.

These three scenarios will describe typical work processes that are deployed in the industry.
The AVEVA Integrated Engineering & Design product portfolio is fully flexible and
configurable to adapt to the various customer and industry needs.

The second scenario is of medium complexity and is likely to be the most common one
within the AVEVA customer base. This scenario will be taken as an initial example to provide
a detailed guide for the implementation of best practices using the AVEVA Integrated
Engineering & Design product portfolio, in particular AVEVA Engineering.

It is assumed that each discipline has distinct responsibilities and one can only modify the
data he/she is responsible for.

Mechanical

Before providing further detailed descriptions of the three scenarios we will explain what we
mean by Status Control, Data Versioning and Data Maturity in this document.

4.1  Status Control, Data Versioning and Data Maturity

Before describing the three scenarios in further detail it is important to agree on terminology
such as Status Control, Versioning and Maturity. This section will define the terminology
adopted throughout this document for the three terms.

4.1.1  Status Control

The term Status Control requires a twofold explanation or definition.

Firstly, the status dataset describes the quality of the information you are working with. The
status is cyclic by nature and could have, for example, the following values: Work in
Progress, Pending Approval, Approved.

Graphically this could be represented as follows:

When one has completed his or her job, the status should change to the next downstream
step/activity within the defined work process. This can be done manually and is referred to
as Promote. On the contrary, when for example, an approver has comments such that part

Work in Progress

Page 6 of 13

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

of the activities need to be redone; this person will Demote to pass the activity back to a
design person for example.

These status conditions are very generic and could apply to a dataset (group of attributes of
the same discipline), as well as to the tagged item itself (described by a group of datasets) or
to any document or deliverable.

The status conditions reflect a company's work processes and should be modelled as such.

Secondly, when considering the tagged item or document, we should also consider another
status condition, namely a tagged item or a document can be deleted. In many cases one
would like to keep a record of the deleted item or document. This is generally referred to as
'logical delete' (or soft delete) as opposed to 'physical delete' (or hard delete) where the
item or document is physically removed from the project.

There could be many reasons for keeping track of a deleted item or document. For example,
ensuring that the tag number allocated to the deleted item isn't used again for another item
(this could create problems if the procurement cycle had already started for that item). Or
the deleted item could be re-instated at a later stage, in which case one wouldn't have to
type all data once again. Or for historical purposes one would like to be able to view what
documents were related to each tagged item at each stage of the project.

Introducing the Deleted Status, the picture would look as follows:

To simplify the implementation descriptions later in this document it has been decided to
only use the statuses Work in Progress, Approved and Deleted for the purposes of describing
the work process.

Work in Progress

Page 7 of 13

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

Now that the status conditions have been described, we can define how the control part
plays a role. Usually, when defining a status of a dataset, tagged item or a document one
also expects to control the type of actions that are authorised to that particular dataset,
tagged item or document once a new status condition has been set. In other words, defining
a rule and role-based behaviour based on status conditions.

Typically, for Work in Progress status a standard user could make any change to the data
contained in the dataset he/she is responsible for. However, once the Approved status is
selected, the following events could be triggered:

1.  Standard users will not be able to modify anything to their dataset

2.  Approvers (generally a senior engineer) will be able to approve/reject the dataset,
but nothing else (they aren't responsible for the data and can only set the status
condition to Approved, Work in Progress or Deleted).

Of course, each status can trigger a number of events, setting a number of restrictions, even
at the lowest possible level. It is possible to set restrictions to individual attributes.

Within the AVEVA Integrated Engineering & Design product portfolio, one can use standard
capabilities, like Status Control to setup and define the various possible statuses reflecting
the company's work processes and DACs (Data Access Controls) to define the level of access
the various roles can have for each defined status condition.

4.1.2  Data Versioning

In order to avoid confusion between 'version' and 'revision' is has been decided that
documents and deliverables (for example a drawing) have revisions and that anything to do
with the data to create these documents and deliverables (for example an object) have
versions. In theory a version of data could be defined at attribute level but, for practical
purposes in this document, versions are applied to groups of attributes or a dataset. It is also
possible to define an overall version for all disciplines related to a tagged item (group of
datasets).

Work in Progress

Page 8 of 13

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

Each company has its own rules for defining versions and revisions. These could be 1, 2, 3,
etc. or A, B, C, etc. Additionally, it is possible to differentiate between Approved versions of
data and Work in Progress ones, for example using an asterisk (*). This would give 1*, 1, 2*,
2, 3*, 3, etc. or A*, A, B*, B, C*, C, etc.

Within the AVEVA Integrated Engineering & Design product portfolio it is possible to show
the status and version of each dataset within the grid. The grid can best be described within
AVEVA Engineering (see screenshot below), but a similar process can be achieved in the
other products within the portfolio.

The grid is like an excel spread sheet as shown above, with one item per row. Typical
capabilities of the grid are; grouping (multiple levels), filtering and sorting, change
highlighting by color, greyed out cells to show read-only mode, calculated fields, etc.

4.1.3  Data Maturity

Based on the same principles as Status Control, it is possible to describe the maturity of
datasets and documents. Within the AVEVA Integrated Engineering & Design product
portfolio it is possible to define multiple status attributes against one dataset or document.
Unlike status, maturity isn't cyclic in nature and identifies the various milestones in a project.
Again, this is based on company specific work processes.

Typical values for Data Maturity could be: For Design, For Review, For Procurement, For
Construction, For Commissioning, etc.

The words have been chosen in such way that when Status and Maturity are combined they
have a meaning.

  Approved For Construction

Work in Progress

Page 9 of 13

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

  Pending Approval For Procurement

  Work in Progress For Review

Within the AVEVA Integrated Engineering & Design product portfolio it is possible to show
the status, version and maturity of each dataset within the grid. Of course, it is also possible
to show the overall maturity of the tagged items based on the individual statuses of their
datasets. Typically, when all datasets have reached the required level of status the tagged
item itself can be marked with the appropriate level of maturity.

4.2  Multi-discipline users on small projects

Earlier in this section it was assumed that each discipline has distinct responsibilities and one
can only modify the data he/she is responsible for. However, it could be possible that one
person has responsibility over more than one discipline. In this case the datasets are still
controlled per discipline with their respective Status conditions and Controls.

However, the required level of interdisciplinary control is less strict than when the teams are
larger and don't have extended responsibility over more than one discipline. This means that
when a person is in charge of all disciplines regarding one tagged item, making a change to,
for example, the process data that has an impact on the mechanical data, he/she will
automatically know what data to change in which discipline. Furthermore, because in
belonging to multiple roles/teams this person wouldn't necessarily have to login twice, but
will be able to do all required modifications at once, the status of the data for
interdisciplinary work becomes less important.

The work environment for this scenario could be represented as below. There is one
environment with distinct spaces for the data of each discipline, and each discipline sharing
the latest version of its data with all other disciplines.

Process
Master
Data

Electrical
Master
Data

Mechanical
Master
Data

The definitions of Status Control, Data Versioning and Data Maturity are still important and
will have to be defined to match the company's work processes. The separation of Work in
Progress data and Approved data for work sharing purposes is not required in this particular
case and simplifies the project setup and definitions. Typically, this doesn't require the use of

Work in Progress

extracts (similar to a local working copy of your data) or publishing the Work in Progress data
once Approved. This will be described in the next section.

4.3  Multi-discipline users on medium/large projects using Status

Control

In this example we will consider project teams that are each responsible for only one
discipline. When projects and project teams are larger we often find that companies
segregate roles and responsibilities. The different disciplines would first work in a dedicated
working environment that is solely visible to them and only when the modifications to the
data are complete will the dataset be Approved, using Status Control capabilities. This is
when the data will be published into the Master Data environment, visible to the other
disciplines.

The dedicated working spaces for each discipline could be represented as follows:

Engineers start work in their working space and when the data is of consistent and of
required quality they will change the status of the dataset to Approved, which will
automatically trigger an event publishing the data to the Approved space (the Master Data
areas in this example).

Each discipline will have a similar working environment.

Unlike the previous example, this scenario allows better control on the data that others are
working with. This is required, because the discipline engineers have distinct responsibilities
and it is preferred to share Approved data with others, instead of ever-changing working
data.

It is believed that this scenario will be the most common one. Therefore a more detailed
description on how to implement this is described in the subsequent sections.

4.4  Multi-discipline users on large projects within Design

Authorities

This scenario provides additional control with respect to historical data in particular. It will
also allow a more granular control of the data that is being shared with the other disciplines.
A historical record of previous versions for all status changes to “Approved” is maintained at
the “Approved” level and a historical record of previous versions for all maturity changes is
maintained at the “Configuration” level (see below).

In the first scenario Work in Progress, Approved and Data Maturity were all stored within the
same space.

The second scenario separates the Work in Progress data from the Approved (with Data
Maturity which is stored in the same space).

Work in Progress

EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

In this third scenario Work in Progress is separated from the Approved data and the Data
Maturity cycle is again separated from the Approved data. The final level could be
considered as a Configuration level.

This gives us three levels of data quality or maturity which could be represented as follows
for each discipline:

This scenario provides new sharing possibilities. For example, within the engineering
community one company could decide to share Approved data with the other disciplines,
but for the design activities this company prefers sharing the engineering data at the
Configuration level. Better and more granular control is the result.

5  Appendix A: Practical Implementation Scenario
This section will describe in more practical details how the second scenario, Multi-discipline
users on medium/large projects using Status Control, could be implemented using the
AVEVA Integrated Engineering & Design product portfolio, in particular AVEVA Engineering.

This scenario is for projects and teams with distinct responsibilities. In this case it is not
desirable to immediately share engineering data that is being modified. Other disciplines
might be busy with a calculation or sizing based on initial information and don't want the
source data (from another discipline) to be changing all the time as data is entered and
saved. For example, when doing a mechanical sizing based on process data, one doesn't
want the reference process data to be changing all the time while still busy doing this sizing
activity.

AVEVA Integrated Engineering & Design provides the users with extreme flexibility and
control when data from other disciplines is accepted. It is very important to note that data is
never pushed from one discipline to another, but instead always pulled from within the
receiving discipline. As such the receiving discipline is fully in control of accepting another's
data. The mechanism by which data is being accepted from other disciplines or even other
applications, within the AVEVA Integrated Engineering & Design product portfolio, is called
Compare & Update. This is an extremely powerful, yet flexible capability.

AVEVA Engineering within the AVEVA Integrated Engineering & Design product portfolio is
the place where multi-discipline data is being evolved and shared with all other disciplines,
Process, Mechanical, Stress, Piping, Electrical and Instrumentation data can be viewed
and/or stored in AVEVA Engineering (whether or not created inside this application). AVEVA
Engineering can also view and link data from P&IDs (called Schematic data) or 3D models
(called Design data), created within the AVEVA Integrated Engineering & Design product
portfolio.

Unlike design data, engineering data needs a more flexible approach as to how it is set up
and used. A PIPE in design can be accessed in a multi user environment. Its design is
controlled by a single discipline. It may have extra UDAs (User Defined Attributes) added to
the standard attributes. These UDAs are again populated by the design team but can come
from external sources such as a spreadsheet.

There is however no fixed hierarchy for engineering tags. The system can be set up to create
any type of tag required for a particular business. Also there are no fixed attributes, so it is
entirely up to the company or project to define which tag types need to be created and what
attributes are required for a particular tag type.

Also the attributes on an engineering tag may need to be populated by several different
disciplines. So unlike a PIPE, a tag may need to be accessed by several teams at the same

Work in Progress

Page 1 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

time. This requires a more flexible approach to the setup and control of the data associated
with a particular tag type.

5.1  Data Control

The level of data access is controlled using the standard AVEVA administration functions for
Modules and Teams.

Data revision is controlled using the standard tools:

  Database Extracts

  Status Controls

  Database Access Controls

An object in the database, for example a PUMP, can be modified in any of the standard
modules. Engineering data is modified in AVEVA Engineering, Schematic data is modified in
AVEVA Diagrams and 3D data is modified in AVEVA PDMS/E3D.

General data access is controlled using standard team membership. Engineering data can be
further controlled by distributing the data for a single object across several databases. This
facilitates the modification of Process, Mechanical and Electrical data by separate disciplines
via their team membership.

The revision of the data being modified and viewed can then be controlled using a
combination of extracts, status controls and Database Access Controls, depending upon how
much control is required. Using the engineering data of an object as an example, mechanical
and electrical data can be placed into two separate databases with the first level of access
controlled by team membership.

The level of control of object data can be setup according to project requirements. For
example, projects not wanting to use extract databases can work with dynamic data from
other disciplines. They will see each other’s data with the usual savework/getwork
mechanism, along with status of the data.

Electrical

Database

Should a project wish to work with other disciplines by only being able to view data that has
been approved, then they will need to setup extracts for working data, and only allow
approved data to be issued to the parent (approved) database.

Each database can have an extract in which the users modify the data.

Electrical

Mechanical

Database

Working

Data

Mechanical
Data

Users working on the extract databases can only issue data up to the parent (Approved)
database if a particular level of status (defined by the administrator) has been set. This
means that users from other disciplines viewing the Master databases are only seeing
approved data.

It is important to stress that, because data is linked between the Engineering database, the
Schematics database and the 3D database, all data objects can be viewed from all sources.

For example in the 3D design environment the user can view or use the data from the
Engineering database. Similarly in the Engineering database the user may want to put the 3D
location onto the line list. This information is obtained directly from the approved 3D
database and will therefore always be up to date. It is of key importance to decide where a
particular piece of data is mastered. It is obvious that the 3D location of an object should be
obtained from the 3D database. However, the specification of a PIPE in 3D can be set in the
3D environment directly but the master source of the data could be either from the
Schematics database or from the Engineering database. Any data discrepancies between the
areas can be compared and highlighted to resolve data inconsistencies.

Work in Progress

Page 3 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

5.2  Project Setup

The level of control that is used on any particular project is dependent upon the size of the
project.

For example, on a small project there may be one team of designers building the 3D model,
another team of engineers and designers preparing the P&IDs and an engineer preparing all
the engineering data. At the other end of the scale on a large project, the 3D Model could be
split into various teams and disciplines across a number of locations using global technology.
The same scenario could be applied to both the P&IDs and the engineering data. It follows
that if the engineering data is distributed across different databases then the engineering
object’s data can also be managed at different locations.

The advantage of the AVEVA solution is the complete flexibility concerning both project
setup and the level of control placed on an object’s data flow.

Project Setup concerns all of the following which will be discussed in detail in the
subsequent sections of this guide:

  Setup of the engineering items and attributes

  Setup of the engineering databases

  Distribution of the engineering data across different databases

  Creation of  extract databases

  Distribution of extracts to different locations

  Setup of status conditions for the engineering items

  Setup of Data Access Controls to control the issuing of data from the extracts based

on status

  Setup of database views

Work in Progress

Page 4 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

6  Appendix B: Engineering Items and Attribute Setup
The data model is completely flexible which makes it possible to create any type of item and
add any attribute to that item. As an example, an Equipment Tag data model will be set up
with relevant data attributes. The example will work through creating a tag item with both
direct and distributed attributes in an attempt to demonstrate all potential scenarios.

Before starting the data model, it is important to have an overview of which attributes are
required and how the attributes will be distributed to enable concurrent, cross-discipline
modification of the tag data.

For this example the new tags will have process data attached directly to the tag and
mechanical and electrical data distributed across two separate databases. This will allow
three different teams to modify data on the tags concurrently.

The attributes that are applied to the tags would most likely be the ones that already exist
on company standard engineering lists and datasheets.

The data model is created in LEXICON, the tags themselves are created in AVEVA
Engineering.

6.1  Tag Type

Every tag type will need a UDET (User Defined Element Type) created to distinguish it from
other tag types. E.g. LINETAG, EQUITAG, VALVTAG etc.

See appendix AA for details.

6.2  Tag Attributes

An EQUITAG can have many attributes. For instance there could be Process, Mechanical and
Electrical attributes. These attributes may be specific to equipment tags or attributes that
can applied to different engineering tags. The attributes themselves are all UDAs (User
Defined Attributes) and should be organised into meaningful groups.

Work in Progress

Page 5 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

On small projects all the attributes can be attached directly to the tag itself but this means
that, like other objects in the database, if one user is modifying the tag, other users cannot
access the tag to write to (1 writer, n readers).

The use of DACs (Data Access Controls) could be used to allow only
certain users to access specific attributes.  But if the process user was
modifying process attributes, that user would stop any other data
from being modified on the tag.

In most projects different departments will need to write attribute data to the tag
concurrently. This means that the attribute data will need to be distributed.

This allows concurrent, multidiscipline access to all the tags.

This allows each section of data to be claimed and modified individually by the relevant
disciplines.

6.2.1  Tag attributes attached directly

All the attributes need to be created as a UDA in LEXICON and these attributes can be
attached either directly to the tag type or binding item.

Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

See Appendix AA for details.

6.2.2  Distributed Attributes

The administrator has to decide how many different teams need to access different attribute
sets on a tag. For instance if there are two other discipline data types (Mechanical and
Electrical) then two separate attribute areas or bound items for each discipline need to be
created.

See Appendix AA for details

6.3  Distributed Schema

The distributed attribute data has to be bound to the tag itself. The tag is the binding item
and the distributed parts are known as the bound attributes.

The point here is to ensure that the binding item and the bound attributes all reside in
different databases, so along with Lexicon, there is a requirement to create databases in
Admin with administrative hierarchies.

See Appendix AA for details

7  Appendix C: Creating the databases and distributed

hierarchy

The control of who can write to the binding item and the bound attributes can now be
controlled by normal team access. Once the distributed schema has been set up then it is
possible to create the databases in the usual manner, create extracts as required and add
the relevant databases into the MDBs.

The only extra requirement is to ensure that when the databases are created that the top
level item is created correctly in the database on creation. See Appendix BB for details.

7.1  User Considerations

During setup, the tags are created in one database by a user with write access to that
database. This means that the user will be able to create engineering tags and modify
attribute data directly associated with the tags, but not be able to modify data or create
bound data for the other disciplines. The users from other disciplines have no rights to
create tags as they are working on the bound data only and therefore can only modify
attribute data on the tags that they have write access to.

Tags can be created using standard functionality such as:-



Import from a spreadsheet

  Compare and Update from Diagrams



Individually

Depending on the level of control required it may be necessary to create another level of
user who has rights to create tags and modify data across all disciplines. This user would
most probably be the engineer who has overall responsibility for the final tag lists/reports.

Appendix D explains how to setup DACs (Data Access Controls) to allow different access to
the tags and their attributes.

Work in Progress

Page 9 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

7.2  Setting up the database access (MDB Structure)

The MDB setup is crucial as to how different disciplines view each other’s data.

7.2.1  No extracts

If the project is not working with extracts then the MDB setup is simple. There is a single
MDB with the engineering databases included.

MDB

Tags & direct process
attributes

Mechanical bound attributes

Electrical bound attributes

This working method would show data from other disciplines immediately after a
savework/getwork. This working method provides no real level of control to the other
disciplines.

7.2.2  Working with extracts

If the project is working with extract databases then the following setup is required.

Extract databases are setup to allow users to modify their engineering data and then issue
that data when appropriate up to the parent (approved) database.

There needs to be an overall approved MDB which contains all the parent (approved)
databases. This is the view of all the tags only showing approved data from all the
disciplines.

issued by the other disciplines. In this case the MDB will contain the extract database of the
