8  Appendix D: Status Control & DACs
At this point the databases are ready to be populated with data. This section explains how to
add status control onto the tags and attributes. If no status control is required then this
section can be ignored.

8.1  Status Control – the principles

Statuses can be attached to any object in the database to indicate how complete, in a
particular phase, an object is. The status has a numerical value, which indicates the
percentage value completeness and a string value, such as ‘Approved’ or ‘Working’ as a
description of the status.

The extent to how status is deployed on a project is based on how much control is required
on data flow.

8.1.1  No Status

On small projects the deployment of status on objects could be regarded as of no benefit so
can be ignored.

8.1.2  Simple status (no extracts)

Some projects may find it beneficial to add status to objects in the database. These are
usually applied to major elements. In the case of engineering tags the bound attributes could
simply have two status conditions to indicate their status e.g. Working and Approved.

Work in Progress

Page 13 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

The tag could have extra status conditions such as Deleted (to allow soft delete) and Issued.
These statuses would be applied using standard functionality by the owning disciplines and
viewed immediately by other disciplines after a savework/getwork.

8.1.3  Status Work Flow

Using the tag status above as an example:

  When a Tag is created it has no status

  When status is applied to the Tag it is given the initial status (Working)

  Once the tag data is all approved the Tag status can be promoted to Approved





If the Tag data needs to be modified again then that status is demoted to Working

If a Tag is approved and ready  for Issue its status can be promoted to Issued

  At any stage a Tag can have its status set to Deleted. This allows a ‘soft’ delete so that
if the tag is re-instated, no data is lost. The status would be set back to Working

Work in Progress

Page 14 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

8.1.4  Simple status (with extracts)

The same status conditions can be used in conjunction with extracts and DACs (Data Access
Controls) to control the flow of data.

Controls could include:

  Only allow the issue to the approved data from the working extract to the approved

database if the status is set to ‘Approved’

  Stop the modification of approved bound data

  Stop the modification of approved or issued tags

8.1.5  More control

Additional controls could include:

  Stop the deletion of Tags (only allow a status to be changed to deleted)

  Stop the modification of status of frozen tags for general users but allow it for

specific power users

  Only allow tags to be approved or frozen until all the distributed attribute data is set

to approved and is not claimed out to an extract

Examples of these DACs are explained in the next section.

See Appendix CC for details

Work in Progress

Page 15 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

8.2  Data Access Controls

Data Access Controls are additional controls that are added to a user’s profile to restrict
access to specific database items and their attributes. If a user is part of a team that owns a
database then that user has write access to the database and can create, delete and modify
data within that database as long as it is not being modified by another user.

DACs are created to restrict the Create, Delete and Modify functions to specific items and
their hierarchies and specific attributes. This allows control at an object level as opposed to
database level. The controls can be configured to for example:-

  Stop users from deleting certain objects such as a Tag Item

  Stop the creation of a SITE

  Stop modification of the POSITION of a SITE

  Stop the modification of an object based on an attribute of the object such as Status

  Stop certain actions such a Flush and Issue being performed unless a certain attribute

has been set on the object such as Status

The DACs are applied to users. Therefore different users within the same team can have
different access to the same objects. Any FREE user is not restricted by DACs at all so it is
usually necessary to have a more senior user to have fewer restrictions placed upon them.
For example a designer cannot modify the status or attributes of an Issued item. However,
for design reasons, it may be necessary to make changes to this item. So the engineer does
have permissions to modify the status of an item back to Working to allow the designer to
make the necessary changes.

DACs are created and applied using the access control assistant in the ADMIN module.

See Appendix DD for details.

Work in Progress

Page 16 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

9  Appendix E: Database view creation
Database views are a key component in viewing and controlling data.  The two key aspects
are as follows:

  The setup of the view for data creation and modification

  The setup to view key data from other sources

9.1   Data Creation and Modification

The view is set up to manipulate the element type to be modified. The members of the view
are all direct attributes of the element type.

In the above example the element type is :EQUITAG with all the direct attributes of the tag
and a status condition.

If items can be created directly in AVEVA Engineering then the view also needs a creation
rule to describe where the tag will be created in the hierarchy and which values have to be
set on creation.

These creation rules are fully configurable. An example of a simple creation rule would be to
enforce the user to input a free format name and an initial status. More complex rules would
enforce the user to input a tag letter, number and suffix to allow the creation of rule based
name.

For details see Appendix EE.

9.2   Viewing associated data

Data associated with the tag can also be added to the database view. This allows the data to
be obtained from various sources. This data may be modifiable depending upon whether the
source database is writable.

Work in Progress

Page 17 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

Such data can include the following:

  Attributes from distributed data

  Status of of the distibuted data

  Attributes of linked data from the schematics database

  Attributed of linked data from the design database

This feature is extremely important as it allows information to be viewed and reported on
from all potential sources of the Engineering, Schematics and Design databases. There is no
requirement to copy information from one item to another. The data is sourced directly
from the information stored on the linked item. If status is also used across all the major
object types, then the status of the information displayed can also be shown giving a
complete view of any item and all its dependencies.

with any other name in the database. It must be unique.

Now return to both the Bound Elements definitions (DSXMBR) created earlier and set the
default home attribute to the newly created Default Homes (DSXHOM).

13 Appendix DD: DACs
The DACs are made up of three parts; Scope, Roles and Access Control Rights (ACRs).

A Scope defines the part of the design to which the Role applies.

The Roles are a list of Permissible Operations (PEROPS)

The ACRs are a combination of a Scope and a Role.

The ACR is attached to a user. When the user performs a task each PEROP of the Role is run
to ensure that the rule of the PEROP has not been broken. If it has, the action is not
undertaken and a warning message is output based on the Error Message of the PEROP.

Work in Progress

Page 44 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

Work in Progress

Page 45 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

14 Appendix EE: Database View creation

Project database views are created in LEXICON.

Create or access a Database View World

Create a database view

EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

Set the User Defined name and the element type to be the item type to be created and
modified e.g. :EQUITAG

Create an attribute column for each attribute that is to be modifiable through the view.

The attribute name is set in the ATTRIBUTE field. Note that it is not necessary to name the
items but it is advisable as it will make the setup a lot easier if they are.

The User Defined Name will be displayed as the column header in AVEVA Engineering.

Note that the Status column uses a Pseudo attribute STVDSC along with the Status definition
name as a qualifier.

Work in Progress

Page 47 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

Note that the attribute columns can be re-ordered by dragging and dropping in the
dictionary explorer. This will then be the default attribute order when the list is displayed in
AVEVA Engineering. This order can be modified later in AVEVA Engineering.

If an item can be created in AVEVA Engineering a Create Rule is required. In the database
view create a create rule.

Important! The owner expression has to be set to the name in the database where the tags
are to be created. Tag items cannot be created under ENGWLD elements, so it is necessary
to manually create an ENGGRP in the relevant database and name it the same as the name
given in the Owner Expression.

Create a set attribute rule for any required attributes that have to be set on creation.

Work in Progress

Page 48 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

If status is deployed a set status rule can be created which automatically sets the initial
status on the new Tag.

The Status Definition Ref refers to the Status Definition for tags.

Work in Progress

Page 49 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

14.1  Viewing data from other sources

To add the bound data to the view, create a new Source Element

Note the PML expression XRQLELE(TYPENAME :EQUIELECATT)

This expression is required to obtain the attributes from the bound data.

Under the new source element create the attribute columns for the electrical UDAs.

Repeat for the mechanical data and drag and drop the source data to order the columns.

Work in Progress

Page 50 of 51

Engineering & Design Systems
Best Practice Implementation Guide
EDS-KNO-001 – V 1.1

This layout can be modified in AVEVA Engineering to customise the layout and group
attributes together under user defined headings.

either right click on the

sign

and click Add

folder right click on the

+Add Users to Group

box against the Username

or a User Group to a folder

folder and select Properties

information.

checking the box.

User Group

or a User Group to a folder

of access you are going to
give that User or Group

assigning them.

Click Save

assigned to working folders
and Class Libraries

MAIRE’S JOURNEY WITH AVEVA

23

NEW CHALLENGES OF THE ENERGY TRANSITION

Green Chemicals plants are powered by renewable energy sources, which are often employed for Hydrogen production by
Water Electrolysis. Hydrogen product can be either used as an energy carrier, or as feedstock for the downstream plants.

Renewable energy sources are often discontinuous and
require a Complex Plant Design Able to Face Frequent
Load Variations

Green
Chemical

Green
Chemical

r
e
w
o
P

Hydrogen Storage is employed to buffer these fluctuations.

t

New challenges arise on the technical front

• The design must account

for a wide range of different

operating conditions and be optimized for load changes

• The operation of the plant must be optimized to minimize
costs and material wastes, managing a time series of
events, based on the system's dynamic behavior and its
response to the fluctuations in power availability

DESIGN OPTIMIZATION OF POWER-TO-X PROCESSES

NEW CHALLENGES OF THE ENERGY TRANSITION

24

01234567Ton/hOUR SOLUTION

ArcHy: Power-to-X Plants
optimizer based on Linear
Programming, that minimizes
the levelized cost of the
desired product

• Technology Selection
• Consumption and

Production Profiles
• Sizing of main units
• Financial model

OPERATION

SIMULTANEOUS
OPTIMIZATION

CONFIGURATION

As process team, we translate the
outcomes of the mathematical optimization
into real industrial processes, designing
a plant that is capable to manage power
fluctuations. In particular, we recognize the
importance of dynamically simulating the
operation of the plant to validate the sizing
and prove the integration of the units.

One of
AVEVATM Process Simulation

the tools that we use for this purpose is

• Seamless transition to dynamics
• Easily customizable
• User friendliness
• Multidisciplinary

Green Chemical [ton]

DESIGN OPTIMIZATION OF POWER-TO-X PROCESSES

OUR SOLUTION

25

010203040506002468101214Hydrogen Storage [ton]Hydrogen Generated [ton]Net ammonia generation [ton]010203040506002468101214Hydrogen Storage [ton]Hydrogen Generated [ton]Net ammonia generation [ton]OPTIMIZING THE SIZING
• During cyclic filling and emptying of the Hydrogen Storage vessels there are
strong temperature variations due to Thermodynamic effects and Heat
Transfer phenomena

• As a result, during filling, the full capacity of the storage cannot be utilized,

because the heating effects cause the stored gas to expand and decrease its
density, therefore taking up more volume. Similarly, during emptying, cooling
effects prevent the complete release of the gas. A Dynamic simulation allows
to keep track of these effects and validate the H2 storage design capacity.

Temperature

Pressure

Time

Heat Transfer

Actual
operating
capacity

Extra capacity for
heating effects

Extra capacity for
cooling effects

“Cushion Gas” for
residual pressure

DESIGN OPTIMIZATION OF POWER-TO-X PROCESSES

OPTIMIZING THE SIZING

26

OPTIMIZING THE OPERATION

Green Chemical Capacity Profile

would

simulation

Optimizing the operation with a fully
dynamic
be
computationally prohibitive. Therefore,
modeFRONTIER, a third-party Multi-
Purpose Multi-Objective optimization
is used to combine dynamic
tool
simulation for the storage with steady-
state simulation for the rest of
the
system.

The operation was optimized to
minimize H2 losses, while targeting
the lowest specific cost of Green
Chemical production, which has been
reduced by 10%, while ensuring
availability
continuous
H2
throughout

timeframe.

DESIGN OPTIMIZATION OF POWER-TO-X PROCESSES

OPTIMIZING THE OPERATION

27

RESULTS

The Power Availability Profile and the Green Chemical Capacity Profile (Optimized Variable) for the optimal Case

The first graph shows a comparison between the Power Availability and the Green Chemical capacity profile, highlighting how the optimization
leads to storing more hydrogen in advance to maintain the operation consistently above turndown during periods of low power availability.

The second graph displays the temperature and pressure profiles of the hydrogen storage unit over time. Heating and cooling effects play a
significant role in influencing these profiles. The pressure increases as the storage unit is filled, when hydrogen is being stored. The
temperature within the storage unit also rises affecting the actual capacity of the storage. And, vice versa, for emptying. The heating and
cooling effects are accounted in the dynamic simulation and a minimum temperature of -74 ºC is achieved. Neglecting these effects would
have led to a shortage of hydrogen of about 15-20% on the last day of operation.

DESIGN OPTIMIZATION OF POWER-TO-X PROCESSES

# SOURCE: vendor_aveva\Composition in ISM Rev 1.3.pdf
---

Composition ISM Rev 1.3

Melinda Hemstead : Data Consultant

March 2021 | Version 1.3

Inheritance
Class Libraries can be composed from
sub–libraries, various standards and information subsets.

Selected components can constitute a layered  stack of hierarchical definitions
resulting in a comprehensive Class Library

The most common ways to create Class
Libraries from components:

• Merging into Active Class Library

• Creating a composition stack
from several fragments of
different origins and versions.

2

|  Copyright © 2017 AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Digital Twin Reference Data Libraries (RDL) Composition Example

Compositions

• Layer 0 – Industry Standard i.e. CFIHOS v1.4 or EIS

• Layer 1 – Customer Corporate Digital Twin RDL

• Layer 2 – Asset Specific Digital Twin RDL(s)

• Plant 1 Digital Twin RDL

• Plant 2 Digital Twin RDL

• Plant 3 Digital Twin RDL

This example of a composition is showing how you can layer CL’s for a
project, allowing for minimum management of information. Maintaining
Class Libraries or Class Library segments individually has many
advantages as an SME can manage their own segments of a Class Library
without effecting others or locking a Class Library so that others cannot
use it. By merging these various segments via Composition, a project can
make use of the elements that are relevant to them, without having to
manage those they’ve inherited, merely managing their own segment.

© 2020 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Creating a Composition

Lock your Class Library for
Editing and go to the
Composition tab via the ‘eye’
icon.

© 2020 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Click on the +Add icon and
choose the Class Library to
add as your Composition

Creating a Composition

© 2020 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Your Composition will show
as added green.

If the Class Library has more
than 1 version available, you
can choose the version from
the Required Version
dropdown.

Viewing a Composition

When you click into Class
folders you can see the
composed Class Library,
shown BLUE

© 2020 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Inherited Attributes against
the Classes

Managing your Class Library

© 2020 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

You can extend the
information in your
Composition locally. You can
add specific Attributes etc to
the Composed Class Library
– these will ONLY add to
your version and NOT the
Composed Class Library.

Managing your Class Library

You make the information in
your Composition obsolete.–
these will ONLY be amended
to your version and NOT the
Composed Class Library.

© 2020 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Managing your Class Library

As you have made a change
you can see this has
highlighted green and the
Class folder itself has been
greyed out to show obsolete
– these will ONLY be
amended to your version
and NOT the Composed
Class Library.

© 2020 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Managing your Class Library

If you find locally you would
be adding a duplicate Class
then best practice would be
to keep the Composed Cl
and remove the local entry –
therefore there will be no
management of that Class

© 2020 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

ABOUT AVEVA

AVEVA is a world leader in industrial software, providing engineering and operational solutions across multiple industries,
including oil and gas, chemical, pharmaceutical, power and utilities, marine, renewables, and food and beverage. Our
agnostic and open architecture helps organizations design, build, operate, maintain and optimize the complete lifecycle
of complex industrial assets, from production plants and offshore platforms to manufactured consumer goods.

Over 20,000 enterprises in over 100 countries rely on AVEVA to help them deliver life’s essentials: safe and reliable
energy, food, medicines, infrastructure and more. By connecting people with trusted information and AI -enriched
insights, AVEVA enables teams to engineer efficiently and optimize operations, driving growth and sustainability.

Named as one of the world’s most innovative companies, AVEVA supports customers with open solutions and the
expertise of more than 6,400 employees, 5,000 partners and 5,700 certified developers. The company is headquartered
in Cambridge, UK.

Learn more at www.aveva.com

Creating Users in Connect

Melinda Hemstead : Data Consultant

July 2021 | Version 1.0

Managing your Connect Account

After logging onto your
Connect account, click
onto the 3 bars against
AVEVA Connect

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

User Management

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Click onto User
Management to access
the management of
your Company’s
Connect account

User Management

In User Management you
can Add and Delete Users
and access all the different
areas of Connect that are
required for this process:
Users
Groups
Roles
Import

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Deleting Users

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

To Delete Users simply
click on the bin icon

Adding Users

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

To Add a User simply
click on the + Add user
icon

Adding Users

Download the Import Template
by clicking on the link.
Complete the template (users tab)
Import it via the Import icon
Further instructions are available
on the instructions tab

If you have a large number of Users to add into your account you
can add these via a load template as accessed via the Import
area. All users will be assigned to a default ‘Users’ group. Users
can be assigned to additional groups as part of the import
process, but these groups must already exist in Aveva Connect.

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Adding Users - Details

Complete the details adding:
Username:
Email Address of the User to be added
Groups:
This dropdown brings through Groups that have been set
up. Add you User to one of these groups that is
appropriate for their privileges
Service:
This dropdown will link to all of the services your company
has on Connect – choose the appropriate Group
Folder:
This connects you to the name of the product in
connection to the Service chosen
Role:
This is where the access level is chosen for the User –
Creator, Author, Reader, Administrator

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Roles - meaning

ISM Reader – Read only
ISM Author – Read and Write access
ISM Creator – Read, Write and Create access
ISM Administrator – All access, User
Management

Click Save to complete the registration

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Creating Groups

ISM Reader – Read only
ISM Author – Read and Write access
ISM Creator – Read, Write and Create access
ISM Administrator – All access, User Management
User – This is the Default Group folder when non
has been specified when importing via the
template

Other Groups can be set up to make management
of your Company Connect account easier to
manage.

Click on the +Add group to add a new Group
To Delete a Group click on the bin icon

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

# SOURCE: vendor_aveva\Configuration Schemas in ISM Rev 1.3.pdf
---

Configuration Schemas in ISM Rev 1.2

Melinda Hemstead : Data Consultant

April 2019 | Version 1.2

Configuration Schema

A Configuration Schema is a way of adding a new section or new tab
into ISM to be applied to specific data requirements you may have.
It contains a unique identifier that makes it reusable across multiple
Class Libraries.

A Class Library is required to be Saved and Locked to create a schema

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Adding a Schema

Click on to the Manage Schema icon.
Right click on the Configuration Schemas header.

Add a unique Id, a Name and Description and click Create

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Schema

The new Schema displays with the
Id, Name and Description you
applied.

Click on Configuration then open
the properties of your schema by
clicking on the down arrow

Schema Properties

Enter the name you wish to display for your schema.

Click on the +Add button under Applicable Class Library
Concepts & Entities – add which area you want to apply the
schema to from the dropdown– in this case Functional Classes

Schema Properties

Under General Properties select Derivable and unselect
Obsolete.

Schema Properties

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Note: if the information you are going to display applies to
more than one area – eg – Functional and Document classes
you can add multiple Class Library Concepts

Adding a Form Section

Create a section that will resemble a Form, managing all the
information you wish to show.
Select +Section
Add a section, completing Id, Name and Description.
Add in the properties by clicking on the down arrow

Note: you can have more than one section

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Form Schema Properties - Section

From Layout Characteristic’s you can choose
to display your Form on the Details page as
a section or as a separate tab. Select your
choice
From General Properties select Derivable (if
not already selected)

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Form Schema Properties - Group

Add your Group by clicking on +Group.
Add the ID, Name and Description
Save with the default settings

Note: you can have more than one Group

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Form Schema Properties – Group Properties

Add your Group Properties by clicking on
+Properties.
Add the ID, Name and Descriptionyou
require and Derivable General Property (if
not already selected)

Note: you can have more than one Group

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Form Schema Properties – 2nd Group Properties

Follow the previous steps
to apply a 2nd Group

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Completed Schema

The completed schema with all groups and properties added

Click on Show Preview to view how the form will appear.

If you want to amend any of the details click on Show Design
View to make any necessary amendments.
You can quickly view the Properties by hovering over the header

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Applying a Schema

Applying a Configuration Schema:

Go to the Manage Configurations icon in the side bar

The ISO Mapping CS configuration node is automatically created
based on the schema definition.

Right click on the ISO Mapping CD node and +Add
Add an Id, Name and Description for your Configuration clicking
Create

Unlock your CL and save the changes – adding a comment into
the audit trail

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Applying and Exporting Schema Information

A Configuration Schema remains available in the context of the CL
until you activate the schema by creating at least one configuration.

Simply Lock your CL, click on a Functional Class and the ISO
Mapping will activate. Add in the information required.

Note:If you Export your CL you must highlight the schema
information in order to add it to the Export.

You can either fill the required details using the exported columns
or apply the details within ISM directly

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Making a Change to a Schema

To make a change to the
Configuration Schema, lock
the CL for Editing and click
on the Manage Schema icon
and click on the schema.
Open Show Design view.

This change is going to be
made on the Layout
Characteristics – the
position of the schema is to
change from a separate tab
to the Details tab.

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Implemented Change

Once you have Saved your
CL and Click on a Class, you
will see that the
Configuration Schema now
appears on the Details tab as
a new section

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA is a global leader in engineering and industrial software driving digital transformation across
the entire asset and operational life cycle of capital-intensive industries.

The company’s engineering, planning and operations, asset performance, and monitoring and
control solutions deliver proven results to over 16,000 customers across the globe. Its customers are
supported by the largest industrial software ecosystem, including 4,200 partners and 5,700 certified
developers. AVEVA is headquartered in Cambridge, UK, with over 4,400 employees at 80 locations in
over 40 countries.

Page 3

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
Welcome to the Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts

Welcome to the Cybersecurity Deployment
Guide - Security Concepts

The following topics outline basic cybersecurity concepts and associated guidelines to support securely
deploying AVEVA products and Industrial Control Systems (ICS) in general.

•  This guide on its own is a presentation of basic cybersecurity concepts coupled with high-level guidelines

that you can use as a roadmap to secure your own systems.

•  This guide is also part of a larger, more detailed cybersecurity deployment guide and serves to define the

concepts and terms used throughout the larger guide.

If you need more information about a specific software component in your system, see the official
documentation for that component. For example, if you need help with your anti-virus software, see the
documentation for configuring that software for your AVEVA component.

What's new in the Cybersecurity Concepts Guide

2025

February, 2025: The cybersecurity concepts guide initial publication. The concepts guide is intended both as a
standalone publication and as an introductory component of a more comprehensive Cybersecurity Deployment
Guide.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 4

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

About cybersecurity concepts

The following topics outline basic cybersecurity concepts and associated guidelines to support securely
deploying AVEVA products and Industrial Control Systems (ICS) in general.

This guide on its own is a presentation of basic cybersecurity concepts coupled with high-level guidelines that
you can use as a roadmap to secure your own systems. This guide is also part of a larger, more detailed
cybersecurity deployment guide and serves to define the concepts and terms used throughout the larger guide.

If you need more information about a specific software component in your system, see the official
documentation for that component. For example, if you need help with your anti-virus software, see the
documentation for configuring that software for your AVEVA component.

AVEVA's approach to securing site networks and ICS software is driven by the following principles:

•  View security from both Management and Technical perspectives

•  Ensure that security is addressed from both IT and ICS perspectives.

•  Design and develop multiple network, system, and software security layers.

•  Ensure industry, regulatory and international standards are considered.

•  Aim to prevent security breaches, supported by detection and mitigation.

These principles are realized by implementing the following security recommendations:

•  Prevent security breaches using the following components:

•  Firewalls

•  Network-based intrusion prevention/detection

•  Host-based intrusion prevention/detection

•  Segregate IT/OT and Plant networks

•  Include a clearly defined and clearly communicated change management policy. For example, tracking and

describing firewall configuration changes.

Note: AVEVA strongly recommends following the guidelines prescribed by the U.S. Department of Commerce
for securing ICS software:

The document "Guide to Industrial Control Systems (ICS) Security" [NIST Special Publication 800-82 Revision
3] (https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-82r3.pdf) provides detailed
information about ICS, typical system topologies, security threats and vulnerabilities, and recommendations
for implementing security measures.

Secure the host

Given the sensitive nature of industrial controls, it is important to secure not only the ICS software, but also:

•  the host on which the ICS software runs

•  the network to which the ICS software is connected

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 5

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

•  the hardware used for the ICS software

Note: The "host" is the Windows computer or Windows Embedded device on which your ICS software is
installed and running.

There are several factors to consider for securing the host including:

•  Access to the host

•  Keeping track of and applying the latest operating system (Windows, Linux, mobile device) updates

•  Keeping the host computer free of viruses and malware

•  Protecting the applications and content on the host

Each of these factors is covered in the following sections.

General guidelines for securing the host

The following are general guidelines for securing the host. More specific recommendations are provided in the
subsequent table. You can refer to these guidelines when planning cybersecurity.

•  Use an account with administrative privileges to install the ICS software, and one without administrative

privileges to run the ICS software.

•  Restrict configuration of ICS to a limited set of users.

•  Consider running the ICS software as a Windows service, if that option is available. If the ICS software is run

as a service, run it as a low privileged virtual service account.

•  Once the host is fully configured and placed in its permanent location, restrict physical access and remote
access to it so that only authorized personnel (for example, system administrators, application engineers,
run-time operators) can use it.

•  Consider disabling or removing physical ports (for example, USB, memory card) that might be used to

connect external storage devices and then transfer data.

Recommended Actions to Secure the Host

Host Component

Recommended Action

Notes

Operating System (Windows)

Check that the Windows operating
system on the host is a version that
is under what Microsoft calls
"mainstream support", which
means Microsoft actively maintains
and releases updates for it.

Older versions of Windows are
under Microsoft "extended
support", which means they are
not actively maintained and
therefore might become vulnerable
without notice.

Automate Microsoft product
updates using Microsoft Windows
Server Update Services (WSUS),
which enables you to manage and
distribute updates to computers on

Before installing Microsoft product
updates ensure they have been
tested and are officially supported
by your ICS software.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 6

Host Component

Recommended Action

Notes

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

your network.

Scan the host. Use both anti-virus
and anti-malware software and file
integrity checking software to
regularly scan the host.

Windows includes Windows
Defender by default, but you may
choose to install and use additional
security software from a reputable
company that scans for more types
of malware or performs other
functions.

AVEVA ICS Software

Check that the AVEVA ICS software
on the host has all the
recommended patches and hot
fixes installed.

See the Technology Matrix for
AVEVA software products,
published by AVEVA's Global
Customer Support (GCS) group.

Content on the host

Protect applications and content on
the host.

Enable Windows Firewall and
configure it to close all ports that
are not used by the AVEVA ICS
software. For more information
about port usage, see "Network
Services and Ports" in
Recommended actions to secure
the network.

Disable Windows features like
remote desktop and file sharing
and remove unnecessary programs
like games and social media.

Restrict access to the files,
databases, registry and other
resources on the host.

Use Windows BitLocker to encrypt
the hard drive of computers that
are either mobile or not located in
a secure facility. However, BitLocker
may impact the performance of
computers.

Use server-class storage (SANs)
infrastructure to avoid storing
sensitive data on mobile devices.

Use Windows authentication for
SQL Server data.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 7

Host Component

Recommended Action

Notes

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

Protect data at rest. In the context
of SCADA and other ICS systems,
data at rest includes stored
configuration data, historical data,
backups, and other static data.

Encrypt sensitive files or drives.
Windows BitLocker Drive
Encryption is available through the
Windows Control Panel and can be
used for whole-drive encryption.

Data at rest is data that is not
currently being used or accessed,
such as data stored on a hard drive,
laptop, flash drive, RAID array,
network attached storage (NAS),
storage area network (SAN), or is
archived/stored in some other way.

Set authorization rights to view
data.

Store data at rest in offline or off-
site locations.

Protect data in transit. In the
context of SCADA and ICS systems,
this encompasses deploying a
project to a run-time node,
transmitting process variables, VTQ
data, and other data that is sent
between nodes in a running,
production system. This includes
alerts and alarms.

Ensure the latest TLS encryption is
used for all communications using
the HTTPS protocol as a best
security practice.

Data protection in transit is the
protection of this data while the
data traveling, including the
following examples:

•  From node to node within a

network

•  From network to network

•  Accessed via internet

•  Transferred from a local storage

device to a cloud storage
device

Protect data in use. In the context
of SCADA and ICS systems, data in
use can apply to databases, such as
those used actively by a historian
or deployed to a run-time node.
This needs to be safeguarded by a
secure transfer channel.

Data in use typically is being
processed or accessed either locally
or remotely.

Use encryption, user
authentication, and identity
management to protect data in
use.

Data in use can place data into
RAM for access and processing by
applications and users, potentially
multiple users across different
computers, mobile devices, remote
terminals, or other devices. It is
especially vulnerable.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 8

Host Component

Recommended Action

Notes

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

Configure encryption in SQL
Server. Enable encrypted
connections for an instance of the
SQL Server Database Engine and
use SQL Server Configuration
Manager to specify a certificate.
The server computer must have a
certificate provisioned. To provision
the certificate on the server
computer, you import it into
Windows. The client machine must
be set up to trust the certificate's
root authority.

SQL Server can use Transport Layer
Security (TLS) to encrypt data that
is transmitted across a network
between an instance of SQL Server
and a client application. The TLS
encryption is performed within the
protocol layer and is available to all
supported SQL Server clients.

•  An extra network round trip is
required at connect time.

•  Packets sent from the

application to the instance of
SQL Server must be encrypted
by the client TLS stack and
decrypted by the server TLS
stack.

•  Packets sent from the instance
of SQL Server to the application
must be encrypted by the
server TLS stack and decrypted
by the client TLS stack.

Secure the network

A typical host computer will have some sort of network access. It is increasingly rare for an ICS device to run as
an entirely standalone device. The host may use the network to communicate with other ICS components such
as controllers, sensors, databases, remote clients, and even other hosts in peer-to-peer relationships. You may
also use the network to manage several ICS devices from a development or supervisory computer.

General guidelines for securing the network

Consider the following networking decisions and see the recommended actions in the following table:

•  Type of connection

•  Network structure

•  Network services and ports

•  Client/Server communication

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 9

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

Recommended actions to secure the network

Network Component

Recommended Action

Notes

Type of connection

Decide how the host will connect
to the network.

Network connectivity has trended
in recent years away from wired
"Ethernet" networks to wireless
"Wi-Fi" networks for both business
and industrial uses.

Network structure

Segment the ICS network. The
network can be either physically or
logically segmented from your
other corporate networks. A
physically segmented network is
the most secure. Network
hardware and all computers and
devices connected to it form a
single closed network with no
physical connection to any other
network

A logically segmented network is
physically connected to your other
corporate networks or to the public
internet. We strongly advise against
connecting to the public internet
other than a trusted, secure
ecosystem provided by your ICS

We recommend against using Wi-Fi
for your ICS network. You do not
have physical control over who or
what might access the network. An
intruder can intercept even a
reasonably secure network and
analyze network traffic and
potentially discover a vulnerability.

If you decide to use Wi-Fi for your
ICS network, enable all access
control features on the WAP
including encryption (WPA/WPA2
or later methods), a strong
password and a list of authorized
MAC addresses. Do not try to
"hide" the Wi-Fi network by
disabling broadcast of the Service
Set Identifier (SSID). Doing so
requires users to perform
additional operations to locate the
network then enter the SSID and
security credentials. This extra
network traffic offers opportunities
for interception and analysis.

Methods to segment the network
include:

•  Using a unidirectional gateway

•  Implementing a Demilitarized

Zone (DMZ) network
architecture with firewalls to
prevent network traffic from
passing directly between the
corporate and ICS networks

•  Having different authentication
mechanisms and credentials for
users of the corporate and ICS
networks.

•  The ICS should also use a

network topology that has
multiple layers, with the most

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 10

Network Component

Recommended Action

Notes

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

Network services and ports

software vendor.

See the diagram following this table
for an example of a segmented
network.

Use firewalls to control traffic
through network services and
ports. Identify services and ports
on your network and configure
firewalls. The topology diagram
following this table provides an
example of using firewalls to
segment the network.

Client/Server communication

Encrypt client/server channels.
Use the latest Transport Layer
Security (TLS) standard. You can still
use the Secure Sockets Layer (SSL)
standard for older applications.

critical communications
occurring in the most secure
and reliable layer.

The documentation for your ICS
software typically includes a list of
network ports commonly used by
the software. Given the nature of
ICS, the list typically includes
services like web, email, file
transfer, external databases, device
drivers, and the ICS software itself
for server-client communications.
Configure the firewalls to open only
those network ports that are in use
by your ICS. Disable all unused
services and close all unused ports.

TLS and SSL use a system of
certificates and keys to digitally sign
the messages sent between the
server and client. When you
configure this sort of
communication, you need to use
one of the following:

•  Self-signed certificates

Issued and signed by the same
application that presents it.
Self-signed certificates are easy
to create and manage, but are
secure only if you control both
the server and the client and
control which certificates are
installed on each.

•  Certificates signed by a Public
Certificate Authority (CA):

Slightly difficult and expensive
to acquire, but more flexible
than self-signed certificates
because you do not need to
control both the server and the
client. If you configure the
server to present a CA-signed

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 11

Network Component

Recommended Action

Notes

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

certificate, the client will accept
the certificate because it
recognizes the Certificate
Authority.

•  Domain-issued certificates or
certificates signed by a Private
Certificate Authority using
systems like Microsoft Active
Directory Certificate Service
(AD CS):

Internal certificates typically
managed by your IT
department. They are issued
and validated by an Active
Directory Certificate Authority.
Domain-issued certificates are
free and can be issued
instantly.

Notes: You need to renew CA-
signed and Domain-issued
certificates at regular intervals.

Encrypted and unencrypted
communications typically use
different network ports.

The following diagram is an example of a segmented network topology:

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 12

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

Note: In no case should your ICS network and devices be directly accessible from the public internet. If there is
some part of your ICS that you want to be accessible, (for example, if you want to view web-enabled HMI screens
from a browser or smart phone), your ICS software should include features that securely pass the necessary
traffic between your ICS network and a public-facing server. Such features can include separate public and
private subnets, private IP addresses, security groups, authentication protocols, user identity managers,
gateways, custom route tables, Access Control Lists (ACLs), and others.

Cloud-based systems

An ICS application can access cloud-based solutions or might itself be hosted on the Cloud. It is important to
mitigate the risks associated with cloud-based access and hosting.

Cloud systems and components will be documented in detail in a future release of this deployment guide. Key
considerations are summarized in the following topics.

Access cloud-based solutions

Many AVEVA applications are now being made available through the Cloud, as both hosted and hybrid solutions.
One of the main risks associated with accessing cloud-based applications is unauthorized access. Connecting ICS
software to Cloud solutions must be done in a secure manner and needs to use secure protocols such as
Transport Layer Security (TLS).

Always maintain data integrity.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 13

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

•  By definition, data integrity is the assurance that your enterprise's data is accurate, complete and consistent

at any point in its lifecycle.

•  Maintain data integrity by safeguarding your data against loss, leaks and corrupting influences.

•  Use data classification to identify data that is sensitive and data that can be made public.

•  Secure computers, storage, and networking to secure the data that is stored and transmitted.

•  Work with your Cloud Service Provider (CSP) to configure users, assign access levels and monitor and control

access.

Cloud-based ICS software

While hosting ICS software on the Cloud provides several benefits such as flexibility, scalability and availability, it
is also vulnerable to security risks such as susceptibility to hacking resulting in damage to your organization's
reputation. It is important to implement a security strategy before you make your ICS software accessible on the
Cloud. For securing ICS software on the Cloud, consider the following:

•  Secure access points by putting in place authentication, monitoring, and support mechanisms.

•  Implement cloud-based, centralized security measures including encrypting communications using TLS.

Note: It is recommended that you review the NIST Cybersecurity Framework for additional information.

Secure systems through authentication and authorization

Typically, ICS software is comprised of many systems, each accessed by a variety of users including engineers,
operators and managers. The level of access that each type of user requires is different. It is necessary to
manage user authentication and authorization to secure the system.

Authentication

Authentication is the process of verifying a user's/system's identity. Authentication can be managed in the
following ways:

•  Within the ICS software through application accounts

•  Through Windows accounts, which can be local to a single computer

•  Through Authentication systems (see the next section for details)

While ICS software allows for user and role management, it can become cumbersome and complicated to
manage many user accounts as employees and roles change. Because of this, use of Windows accounts and user
groups is generally preferred.

Authentication systems

Authentication systems such as Active Directory and Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP), referred to as
authentication servers, are a repository of and provide centralized management for all system accounts and
individual user accounts. An authentication protocol is used for all communication between authentication
servers and the user or server requesting authentication.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 14

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

Even though use of authentication systems provides improved scalability, the following factors must be
considered depending upon the size and complexity of your operations:

•  It is important that the authentication servers are highly secured.

•  The authentication server system creates a single system for managing all system accounts. Therefore, it is

required to be available at all times. To ensure minimal disruption during an emergency, redundancy must be
considered.

•  Permit caching of user credentials only for users who have authenticated their identity recently.

•  Networks that support the authentication protocol must be reliable and secure to assist in trouble-free

authentication.

It may also be worthwhile implementing two-factor authentication using additional applications such as PingID.

Authorization

Authorization is the process of providing the correct level of privileges to users by applying access rules to
authenticated users, systems (HMIs, field devices and SCADA servers) and networks (remote sites' LANs).

Manage users and groups through windows

When you configure security, you may choose to do one of the following:

•  Keep the configuration local to a single application.

•  Share the configuration between multiple applications.

•  Manage the configuration as part of the network domain (for example, using Active Directory). This option
typically allows users to have the same user account for the network, the host, and the ICS software. Using
Active Directory gives you the following advantages:

•  A centralized repository for user and group data, enabling effective implementation of security policies

and procedures.

•  Provides a single point of access to all network resources after the user is identified and authenticated.

To manage users and groups:

•  First define a specific role for each group, and then configure the group privileges to fit that role.

•  Groups may overlap, but it is often better to have clearly separate groups and then assign individual users to

multiple groups, if necessary.

•  Set or change the password for the ICS software's default user (e.g., "guest").

•  Define stringent password policies to force users to create strong passwords. Enforce mandatory password

updates on a regular basis.

Manage users and groups through ICS software

Your ICS software should have a built-in security system that controls who may use the software and what
privileges they have.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 15

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

Users should be assigned permissions that determine what each user is authorized to do within the ICS system.
Permissions can be managed either on a per-account basis or on a group basis by making use of roles. Group or
role-based access control is preferred as it greatly simplifies management. Users can be moved from one role to
another as the organization's needs change and can also be members of multiple roles if required.

Each user should have their own user account with a unique username and a strong password. The user account
can then be assigned to one or more groups.

These accounts should always be assigned the least privileges necessary to perform their functions. Accounts
with Windows Administrator permissions should be reduced to the minimum, and typically only used to install
and configure the software. Likewise, accounts with SQL Server SysAdmin privileges should be reduced to the
minimum, and typically only used to install and configure the software.

In most cases, the ICS software will allow associating Windows Groups with roles within the product. While
defining and assigning roles, consider the following:

•  Roles should be defined to have the least privileges necessary for their functionality.

•  Roles should be limited to a single purpose to simplify the permissions assigned to them.

•  Users can be members of multiple roles if necessary.

Plan for contingencies

Incidents are inevitable. It is, therefore, important to develop a strategy to detect an incident quickly and
respond to it in a timely manner to minimize loss and protect your system. An organization must consider
contingencies arising from incidents such as fire, flood and so on, and those arising from failure of hardware or
software components. Cyber attacks such as ransomware are becoming more common and must also be
considered.

An organization should have contingency plans in place to cover the entire range of failures and eventualities.
Employees should be trained and be familiar with the contents of the contingency plans.

As part of planning for contingencies, it is important to establish a site, physically separated from the central one,
that has replication capability. Doing so will ensure the integrity of an operational system where the central site
is at risk from fire, floods or other disasters. The replication capability includes having duplicated hardware and
requires software configuration and key state information to be periodically propagated from the central site to
the recovery site. Each recovery scenario is unique, so it is important to consult with system integration experts
regarding the design of communications equipment, hardware and the configuration of the software.

Protecting the data stored in your system is also of paramount importance. Full and incremental backups must
be scheduled on a regular basis. Backups should be verified by running tests to restore from backed up data.
Backups should be stored offline so that they are safe from cyber attacks such as ransomware.

Organizations should also have business continuity and disaster recovery plans that are similar to contingency
plans. These plans are covered briefly in the sections to follow.

Audit and log

As part of implementing security for ICS software, it is important to incorporate auditing and logging activities on
various systems and networks.

Auditing and logging provide information on the current state of your ICS and help to ensure that the system is
functioning as expected. If an incident occurs, you can use the activity logs to trace the origin of the incident to a

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 16

computer, user, or network. Auditing and logging can also help with troubleshooting issues.

If you are connecting to cloud-based solutions, audit all virtual machines (VMs) to ensure data integrity.

Cybersecurity Deployment Guide - Security Concepts
About cybersecurity concepts

Plan business continuity

Business continuity planning addresses strategies to maintain or re-establish production in the event of any
disruption. These disruptions may be caused by a natural disaster (flood, earthquake, etc), by an intentional or
unintentional man-made event (arson, operator error, power outage, etc), or by system failure.

Depending upon the duration of the potential ICS application outage caused by a disruption, operational
recovery plans for short-term outages and disaster recovery plans for long-term outages must be formulated. It
is also important to employ physical security for areas of a production site that house data acquisition and
control systems that might have higher-level risks. Your business continuity plan should specify system and data
recovery procedures for your systems. Once the recovery procedures are documented, a schedule should be
developed to test the recovery procedures. Particular attention must be paid to the verification of backups of
system configuration data and product or production data. The procedures should be reviewed periodically.

If you are accessing cloud-based solutions, ensure that systems are always available. In case of a disaster,
services should switch to a new physical location to provide continued service.

Plan disaster recovery

A Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP) is a set of procedures to protect and recover an IT infrastructure in case of a
disaster. It contains the procedures to follow before, during and after a disaster. Disasters can be natural,
environmental, or man-made (intentional or unintentional).

A DRP is essential for continued availability of the ICS, and should cover the following:

•  When the DRP should be activated depending upon an event, its duration, and its severity.

•  Detailed course of action for operating the ICS manually until external connections are secured.

•  Personnel responsible for each procedure.

•  Processes for securely backing up data and restoring it. This should cover:

•  Requirements for building redundancy.

•  File backup procedures.

•  Frequency of backups.

•  Storage mechanism for full and incremental backups.

•  Safe storage of installation media, license keys and configuration information.

•  List of individuals responsible for performing, testing, maintaining, and restoring backups.

•  List of personnel with physical and virtual access to the ICS.

•  Detailed configuration information about the components of the ICS.

•  Schedule for testing the DRP.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 17

™

Tel +44 (0)1223 556655

www.aveva.com

To find your local AVEVA office, visit
www.aveva.com/en/about/about-aveva/locations/

UK

Digital Transformation at HPCL Visakh Refinery:
Implementation of EPDDMS

Presented By:
Jay Kumar Shah, Principal Consultant, Presales, AVEVA
Murali Mohan Sarayavalasa, Chief General Manager-Maintenance, Visakh Refinery
Sridhar Vanapalli, Chief Manager-Maintenance
Aditya Sankar CV, Manager-MES

Introduction

Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Limited (HPCL) is a Maharatna company in the energy business with two
refineries, one on the West Coast (Mumbai) and the other on the East Coast (Visakhapatnam).

The refinery at Visakhapatnam, called Visakh Refinery (VR), was the first East Coast Oil refinery commissioned as
Caltex Oil Refining India Ltd. (CORIL) in 1957 with a capacity of 0.675 MMTPA.

Taken over by Government of India in 1976 and merged with HPCL in 1978 with an installed capacity of 1.5 MMTPA.

The initial capacity of 1.5 MMTPA has expanded to 15 MMTPA over the last few decades thru multiple expansion
projects, Revamps  and commissioning of Visakh Refinery Modernization Project (VRMP) this year.

Problem Statement

In the last 6 decades, Visakh refinery has undergone many modifications and expansions. These modifications
were done by various EPCs and most of the as-built documentation hand over is in the form of hard copies.

Over the period, this information has undergone a huge change in field due to various revamps and
modifications, which may or may not have been reflected in documentation.

This created challenges like:

Hard copy
documents in
scattered form

Incomplete as-built
documentation

Lack of clarity on
completeness of
proj. documentation

MOCs/revamps not
always updated
leading to incorrect
procurements/
execution delays/
re-works

Storage of
information in hard
copy format take
huge space and is
prone to damage,
misplacement and
maintenance issues

Retrieval takes long
time leading to
increased project
lead times

Initiative Implemented

Creation of digital assets like intelligent 3D models, smart P&IDs, documents and master tag database for
legacy refinery units in phased manner using AVEVA  Unified Engineering. This digital initiative included
following activities:

• Laser scanning of units in field and creation of intelligent 3D models, from which, GA drawings, ISO & 2D

drawings are generated seamlessly

• Creation of intelligent Process & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs), Instrumentation and Electrical

diagrams, Master Equipment Tag Database

• Integration of the digital assets, ensuring data consistency between 3D and 2D data.

Initiative Implemented

Further, Asset Information Management (AIM) solution called Electronic Project Data and Document Management
System (EPDDMS) was implemented to host all the digital plant information in a single platform using AVEVA  Asset
Information Management.

Tag based Asset management system

1

Centralized digital
repository

Enables visualization
irrespective of source

6

5

EPDDMS

4

2

3

Enables Web access,
Quick & Data centric

Ensures data integrity thru
Validation and Reporting

Communicates with all asset related documents

Initiative Implemented

EPDDMS

Project Production Server

Validated
Information

EPDDMS Team

EPDDMS – Validation Server

LSTK-1

LSTK-2

EIL

HPCL legacy
documentation

Benefits Realized

Improved data validation through multiple sources,
ensuring consistency and compliance.

Seamless accessibility to accurate and validated
information

Enhanced Design and Engineering

• Higher accuracy, quicker engineering of large scale projects / revamps
• Reduced manual errors, rework, and associated costs.

Shorter lead times for MOC and Maintenance

• Accurate fabrication of piping spools & in-kind replacements of

components

• Ensuring compliance to scheduled timelines

Faster data retrieval, reducing time spent searching for
critical information.

Enhanced decision-making with reliable, up-to-date
data.

Shorter lead times for MOC and Maintenance

• Reduction in

Engineering Hours

25-30%

• Savings in information

retrieval time

>50%

• Reduction in reworks
during execution due
to lack of information
/ misinformation

80-90%

Benefits Realized

BEFORE

AFTER

Information is available in scattered, person
dependent manner

Information is available in a centralized
platform at the touch of a button

High lead time for gathering required
information (minutes to hours)

Reduced lead time for gathering required
information (seconds)

Low clarity on information correctness

High clarity on information correctness

More prone for project delays/reworks due to
lack of information correctness / completeness

Less prone to project delays/reworks due to
improved information reliability

Information available in hard copies which are
prone to damage and misplacement

Information available in soft format in secure
servers, enabling seamless access

Unique Features of the Initiative

Integration with all kinds of data (2D/3D) of any kind of formats (of different OEMs) thru inbuilt gateways.

Integration with other Enterprise platforms like SAP (ERP system), LIMS (Laboratory Information
Management System) and Process Historians

Intuitive and user-friendly interface, enhancing user adoption and efficiency

Robust security measures to safeguard sensitive refinery data thereby ensuring compliance with
Industry regulations

Easily scalable with the refinery's growth

Other Digital Transformation Initiatives
Implemented
Online Corrosion Monitoring thru IIoT sensors

• Real time monitoring of asset health, enabling preventive maintenance
• Pre-emptive failure detection and reduction in unplanned shutdowns

AR/VR/MR based Training and Simulation for FCHCU

• Virtual Reality (VR) based operator training simulator, containing 25 Nos. scenarios of

FCHCU (start-up / emergency / contingency categories)

• Augmented Reality (AR) / Mixed Reality (MR) based maintenance module for

assisting maintenance engineers and remote OEM / SME support

Digital Turnaround (TA) Management

• Real-time monitoring of Turnaround activities through the use of intrinsically safe

mobile devices

• Enhanced task management, Analytics, critical path identification and prompt

resolution of issues

Thank You

Any Questions?

ABOUT AVEVA

AVEVA is a world leader in industrial software, providing engineering and operational solutions across multiple industries,
including oil and gas, chemical, pharmaceutical, power and utilities, marine, renewables, and food and beverage. Our
agnostic and open architecture helps organizations design, build, operate, maintain and optimize the complete lifecycle
of complex industrial assets, from production plants and offshore platforms to manufactured consumer goods.

Over 20,000 enterprises in over 100 countries rely on AVEVA to help them deliver life’s essentials: safe and reliable
energy, food, medicines, infrastructure and more. By connecting people with trusted information and AI -enriched
insights, AVEVA enables teams to engineer efficiently and optimize operations, driving growth and sustainability.

Named as one of the world’s most innovative companies, AVEVA supports customers with open solutions and the
expertise of more than 6,400 employees, 5,000 partners and 5,700 certified developers. The company is headquartered
in Cambridge, UK.

Learn more at www.aveva.com

Five reasons to take a data-centric approach from engineering to operationsWhat this looks like in practiceAVEVA’s engineering  portfolioCreating value at every step of the asset life cycleTop five reasons to take a data-centric approachCapitalizing on engineering dataFive reasons to take a data-centric
approach from engineering to operations

3

Capitalizing on engineering data

Industrial organizations that capitalize on engineering
data can use it to create a feedback loop for continuous
improvement. But in order to do so, organizations first
need to take a data-centric approach to designing,
building and operating facilities.

Because engineering software can form the
cornerstone of a digital twin, engineering data is
a critical part of understanding how well a facility
is operating and if it’s operating as designed. This
understanding becomes fundamental to informing
facility updates and future builds.

Using engineering data throughout a facility’s life cycle creates value

Drive
Innovation
through
operations
feedback

Owner’s digital twin:
Aggregate and visualise in context
(facility/fleet)

EPC’s digital twin:
Aggregate and visualise in context
(facility/fleet/all end users)

Enable digital
twin value
by optimizing
and enhancing
operations

Engineer (execute projects)
•  Better designs and models
•  Monetize operational insights
•  Reduce carbon footprint

Operate
•  Faster project handovers
•  Enable the knowledge worker
•   Native asset intelligence for

optimal operations

What this looks like in practiceAVEVA’s engineering  portfolioCreating value at every step of the asset life cycleTop five reasons to take a data-centric approachCapitalizing on engineering dataFive reasons to take a data-centric
approach from engineering to operations

4

What this looks like in practice

A digital thread from engineering to operations

In typical, traditional project asset life cycles, engineering hands off
to operations in a series of gated, distinct events, and the facility
remains operational until decommissioning. It’s a segmented linear
timeline. A well-formed digital twin built on verified engineering data
offers the opportunity to turn this process into a feedback loop.

A digital twin created in the design phase enables operators to
use that multi-dimensional representation as they are building
the facility. This means operators could use the digital twins to
identify potential design flaws, optimize layouts and improve
overall functionality. Different departments or external partners can
collaborate in real time when accessing and reviewing project data
using the digital twin.

What this looks like in practiceAVEVA’s engineering  portfolioCreating value at every step of the asset life cycleTop five reasons to take a data-centric approachCapitalizing on engineering dataSimulating the construction process
with a digital twin can help identify
the most efficient construction
sequence, taking information directly
from design to minimize delays.
Furthermore, operators could use
it to analyze potential risks and
challenges during construction,
allowing for proactive planning.

Utilizing the digital twin to monitor the
usage and movement of construction
equipment enables optimized
deployment strategies. Operators can
also use the living data to simulate
the facility and train operators to
operate it—before the construction
even finishes.

Five reasons to take a data-centric
approach from engineering to operations

5

Beyond construction, the digital
twin can continue to be valuable
for maintenance and operations
planning. It can provide a
comprehensive view of the facility,
setting the operator up for predictive
maintenance and optimizing
operational workflows.

As a dynamic and interactive
model, a digital twin of the facility
empowers engineers to make
informed modifications throughout
the various stages of its life cycle.
This process can involve simulating
different scenarios within the digital
twin to understand the potential
impact of modifications. Or it can
even be used to simulate the impact
of new regulatory requirements on the
facility. Engineers can then propose
modifications to ensure compliance
with updated regulations.

What this looks like in practiceAVEVA’s engineering  portfolioCreating value at every step of the asset life cycleTop five reasons to take a data-centric approachCapitalizing on engineering dataFive reasons to take a data-centric
approach from engineering to operations

6

This data-driven approach to industrial projects allows organizations to make informed choices for ongoing construction
activities and future projects. Engineers can use the digital twin to iteratively design and optimize the layout, structure
and systems of future facilities.

(facility/fleet)

What this looks like in practiceAVEVA’s engineering  portfolioCreating value at every step of the asset life cycleTop five reasons to take a data-centric approachCapitalizing on engineering dataAVEVA’s engineering portfolio

AVEVA engineering tools allow organizations to simulate a facility and then
engineer that information into a 1D, 2D, or 3D space. We’ve designed our
software to enable a digital thread that spans the capital expenditure process
into operations and create the foundation for a digital twin.

plant design solution that enables

AVEVA™ Electrical and Instrumentation

and instrumentation elements.

AVEVA™ Process
Simulation is a software
solution that enables

simulation of industrial
processes. It supports
process design,
optimization and control
system testing, allowing
engineers to analyze and
enhance the performance
of complex industrial
processes.

instrumentation diagrams (P&IDs). It
helps in capturing and maintaining P&ID
information throughout the project.

Five reasons to take a data-centric
approach from engineering to operations

10

For facility owners, taking a data-centric
approach provides an unprecedented
view of how a facility is operating
against the engineering designs that
preceded it. Operators can get a facility
up and operating faster to provide time
to value, avoid wasted effort transferring
data between documents, and create a
feedback loop to continuously improve
engineering designs.

# SOURCE: vendor_aveva\EDF_  What is nuclear hybrid digital model_.pdf
---

OCTOBER 2024

What is nuclear hybrid
digital model?

Or “La Maquette Numérique Hybride”

Thomas Arduino (EDF DIPDE) | Aymen HASSOUN (AVEVA)

The presentation will be accessible on the event website.
Kindly refrain from taking photos or recording videos of the
content during the presentation.

Thomas ADUINO & Aymen HASSOUN
AVEVA WORLD 2024 – 16/10/2024

Engineering Support and
Environmental Services
Division

Division de l'Ingénierie du Parc et De l'Environnement

Summary

1. Presentation of Speakers
2. A little bit of history
3. DISC and DPNT organisation
4. The role of DIPDE
5. High Impact worksites for DIPDE
6. How we manage modifications of nuclear plants
7. Build the future of work
8. To summarise
9. Our Future with AVEVA Unified Engineering and CONNECT

Fleet Engineering, Decommissioning and Environment Division (DIPDE) - ©EDF 2024 - This document is the property of EDF and may not be distributed without written permission.

4

1. Presentation of speakers

I'm Thomas Arduino. I have a degree in mechanical
engineering from Ecole des Mines of Saint Etienne. I
started working for EDF 9 years ago as a nuclear
process nuclear trainer at the Tricastin power plant. At
the end of 2019, I joined DIPDE at Marseille to manage
IT projects involving 3D data and point clouds.
I worked with Aymen in EDF during 3 Years

I am Aymen Hassoun, I have a Master’s in construction,
engineering from the University of Cergy-Pontoise, and
today I am a Senior CAD Applications Engineer at AVEVA
France, I am based in Paris, with extensive experience in
3D transformation projects and technical support for
AVEVA tools. He has worked with notable companies like
EDF and ALLIA, before joigning AVEVA.

Fleet Engineering, Decommissioning and Environment Division (DIPDE) - ©EDF 2024 - This document is the property of EDF and may not be distributed without written permission.

5

2. A bit little of history

One of France's distinctive features is the standardization of its nuclear
power plants: all 56 PWR reactors use the same technology and are
technically similar. They are located at 18 nuclear power plant sites, with
each plant comprising between 2 and 6 reactors (or “tranche”).

Reactors are classified according to their model - known as a “tier” - and
the electrical power they deliver: 900 megawatts electrical (MWe), 1300
MWe and 1450 MWe.

There are 32 900 MWe reactors: 4 CP0 reactors (4 at Bugey), and 28 CPY
reactors (4 at Tricastin, 6 at Gravelines, 4 at Dampierre, 4 at Blayais, 4 at
Chinon, 4 at Cruas and 2 at Saint-Laurent).

The 20 1,300 MWe reactors are divided into two levels: the P4 level with 8
reactors (4 at Paluel, 2 at Saint-Alban and 2 at Flamanville) and the P'4
level with 12 reactors (2 at Belleville sur Loire, 4 at Cattenom, 2 at
Golfech, 2 at Nogent sur Seine and 2 at Penly).

There are 4 1,450 MWe reactors in the N4 range (2 at Chooz and 2 at
Civaux).

Map of nuclear plants

Fleet Engineering, Decommissioning and Environment Division (DIPDE) - ©EDF 2024 - This document is the property of EDF and may not be distributed without written permission.

6

3. DISC and DPNT Organisation

Engineering and Supply
Chain Division
(DISC)

Nuclear & Thermal
Generation Division
(DPNT)

Management
DPNT

new nuclear power
plants

nuclear power plants in
operation

(National Center of
